          "result": "# 【0†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file0\nMclick Target: \"11:0\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Jhace wasn’t in the bowl anymore.\r\n[L2] She noticed that absence the way she noticed missing stairs—by almost stepping where something should have been.\r\n[L3] The emblems on coats and banners had finished settling. Square near the front. Loop nearer the lanes. Drop along the water tables. Fracture pushed outward, closer to the exits.\r\n[L4] That wasn’t how they’d been.\r\n[L5] Chloen didn’t know how she knew the difference. She just knew the shapes had moved the way animals moved when you weren’t watching them directly.\r\n[L6] A brace captain called for hands near the west rib. Chloen drifted with the crowd until she wasn’t near Tiffani anymore, then stopped.\r\n[L7] No one noticed.\r\n[L8] That was the trick.\r\n[L9] She stood where two rope lanes crossed and watched how people flowed around the knot. One lane moved faster. The other slowed and backed up. People grew impatient and leaned, and the lean pushed the timing off by half a beat.\r\n[L10] Half a beat mattered now.\r\n[L11] Chloen stepped sideways.\r\n[L12] Just one pace. Not dramatic. Just enough to stand where the knot wanted to form.\r\n[L13] The next pull came.\r\n[L14] People hesitated. The lane loosened. The knot didn’t catch.\r\n[L15] No one fell.\r\n[L16] No one noticed her.\r\n[L17] Chloen’s heart thudded too fast, then slowed when the slack phase came like it was supposed to.\r\n[L18] She hadn’t meant to do anything.\r\n[L19] She just didn’t like how the ropes were talking to the crowd.\r\n[L20] She drifted back toward the stone flats and found the low wall again. Sat. Hugged her elbows. Checked her mouth.\r\n[L21] Still no metal.\r\n[L22] That was worse.\r\n[L23] Tiffani passed close enough that Chloen could hear the pencil scratch. The graphite moved cleanly now, not skirting smears, not hesitating.\r\n[L24] Chloen wanted to say something. Anything.\r\n[L25] She didn’t.\r\n[L26] Across the stadium, Eries moved among the lanes without hurry. People made space for her without being told. She nodded. She listened. She touched shoulders lightly and left them straighter than before.\r\n[L27] She didn’t look at Chloen.\r\n[L28] Which meant she knew exactly where Chloen was.\r\n[L29] The bells rang notice cadence again—short, short, long—and this time Chloen breathed shallow before she remembered she was supposed to.\r\n[L30] Her body had learned faster than she had.\r\n[L31] She pressed her thumb into her palm until it hurt, just to make sure pain still worked the same way.\r\n[L32] It did.\r\n[L33] Good.\r\n[L34] A woman nearby whispered, “It’s better already,” and someone else answered, “Thank the Crown,” and a third said, “Thank her,” without needing to name who.\r\n[L35] Chloen watched the words move through the air like dust settling into cracks.\r\n[L36] She licked her teeth again.\r\n[L37] Nothing.\r\n[L38] She imagined the taste was still there, just buried deeper. Like grit ground into stone so fine you didn’t feel it until later.\r\n[L39] Chloen slid off the wall and followed the edge of the stadium where shadows cut the lines and the bells sounded farther away.\r\n[L40] She didn’t know what would happen next.\r\n[L41] She only knew this:\r\n[L42] If the world stopped hurting you right away, it didn’t mean it was healed.\r\n[L43] Sometimes it meant you’d stopped feeling the damage.\r\n[L44] Chloen kept walking, quiet and uncounted, while the city learned how to behave.\r\n[L45] Epilogue — Corlexi\r\n[L46] Corlexi did not return to the floor.\r\n[L47] He took the side stair instead—the one cut narrow for servants and clerks, stone worn smooth by centuries of people who were never meant to be seen leaving power. The bells were still ringing when he reached the landing, their cadence settling into something regular enough to pretend it had always been that way.\r\n[L48] He paused with one hand on the rail.\r\n[L49] Listened.\r\n[L50] The doubled hum had stabilized. Not vanished.\n# 【1†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file1\nMclick Target: \"11:1\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The tremor tried to rise.\n[L2] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\n[L3] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\n[L4] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\n[L5] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\n[L6] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\n[L7] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\n[L8] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\n[L9] His sternum was already too awake.\n[L10] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\n[L11] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\n[L12] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\n[L13] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\n[L14] And now he knew it could be done.\n[L15] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\n[L16] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\n[L17] Not because there was nothing to write. Because there was too much, and the act of recording would change what people thought they were doing. If she raised graphite now, they’d start performing their panic for the page. They’d turn grief into testimony.\n[L18] She wasn’t ready to be that kind of authority.\n[L19] The upper terrace had become a sorting place.\n[L20] Not formally or by decree—by physics.\n[L21] Rubble moved downhill when it could. People moved uphill when they could breathe. Anyone who could still walk drifted toward the wider stone flats where the terraces broadened and the parapets were intact enough to lean on. The broken streets below funneled survivors toward this ledge the way water found low points.\n[L22] Volunteers—if you could call them that—had formed lines. Hands passed stone from hand to hand. Smaller pieces went into baskets. Larger slabs were propped with scavenged timber and wedge bars. Someone had tied a length of rope across a crack in the paving to keep the crowd from stepping onto a section that shifted on the doubled pulse.\n[L23] The work sounded ordinary.\n[L24] Stone scraping. Breath. A cough. A child’s thin crying. A man’s voice calling names into the air until his throat went raw.\n[L25] Ordinary work inside an impossible day.\n[L26] Tiffani moved through it without stepping into anyone’s line. Her eyes tracked loads and fractures the way her hands tracked stars. She counted by seeing.\n[L27] One: a retaining wall seam opened a hair each pulse.\n[L28] Two: a lintel with a new shear line, chalked in red by someone who knew what “don’t stand here” meant.\n[L29] Three: a brace post that had set crooked after liquefaction—compensating now, but only by being loud about it.\n[L30] She was not the only one paying attention. That was the most surprising thing.\n[L31] A woman with a carpenter’s hammer had marked a series of doorframes with wax. A boy with a long pole tapped stones and listened for the hollow note of delamination. Someone—older, with a scarf over their mouth—had turned a smashed cart into a supply table and was rationing water in cups like it mattered, because it did.\n[L32] Tiffani felt the doubled hum in her knee with every step. It registered as a timing problem: the joint wanted one rhythm and the ground insisted on two.\n[L33] She adjusted her stride to the slack phase. It made walking possible.\n[L34] She did not allow herself to enjoy that small mastery.\n# 【2†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file2\nMclick Target: \"11:2\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] That explained the slate, the chronometer in her head, the way she looked at the Rift like it was a problem that could be measured into submission.\n[L2] Jhace looked down at the girl.\n[L3] Chloen’s chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. Her hands were still dirty. Counts like it’s keeping her upright.”\n[L25] Tiffani held that for a beat, then nodded once. “Not mine,” she said. “Not from the glass-house.”\n[L26] “Do you know who she is?” Jhace asked.\n[L27] “I know what she is,” Tiffani said, and her voice went flatter. “A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn’t break.”\n[L28] Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines forming—hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. He let the implication settle where it wanted.\n[L29] Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.\n[L30] He didn’t like that. He also understood it.\n[L31] “That’s three of us,” Jhace said, more to himself than them. “One who holds. One who measures. One who survives.”\n[L32] Chloen’s mouth tightened. “I didn’t—”\n[L33] “I know,” Jhace cut in, not unkind. “You did.”\n[L34] She stopped. Swallowed. That taste was getting old, even for him, and he hadn’t been the one under the slab.\n[L35] Tiffani’s gaze went back to the horizon. “The others will start asking,” she said.\n[L36] “Let them,” Jhace said. His hands flexed once, then stilled on the wedge bar. “But if that slate woman shows up again—”\n[L37] Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate once. Controlled. A marker.\n[L38] “—you tell me,” Jhace finished.\n# 【3†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file3\nMclick Target: \"11:3\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] That explained the slate, the chronometer in her head, the way she looked at the Rift like it was a problem that could be measured into submission.\n[L2] Jhace looked down at the girl.\n[L3] Chloen’s chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. Her hands were still dirty. She’d stopped wiping them like it mattered.\n[L4] He didn’t ask gently.\n[L5] “And you.”\n[L6] She blinked, then said, “Chloen.”\n[L7] The name sat wrong in his mouth for a beat—too soft for the day it had been forced into.\n[L8] He nodded anyway. “Chloen.”\n[L9] He let the wedge bar rest against his thigh. The tremor in his fingers tried to start up and he ignored it by giving his hands something to do.\n[L10] “Good,” he said, and meant: good you’re alive.\n[L11] He didn’t add the rest. He didn’t know how to say it without making it sound like ownership.\n[L12] Tiffani’s eyes were still on the horizon. “You’re Jhace,” she said.\n[L13] He looked at her.\n[L14] “I heard it,” she added, because she knew how that sounded.\n[L15] Jhace’s jaw worked. “Yeah.”\n[L16] A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Dust lifted. Copper-gold grit along the slate edge rose a hair and settled again.\n[L17] Jhace watched it happen and took the metallic hit at the back of his tongue like a reminder. He swallowed. Stupid copper.\n[L18] He forced his attention away from his own body and back onto the practical.\n[L19] “There’s a woman,” he said. “Slate. Chalk. Counting.”\n[L20] Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched once at the word counting. Not interest—recognition.\n[L21] “I saw her twice,” Jhace went on. “Once after the slab. Once again before the fire line. She wasn’t looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands.”\n[L22] Chloen’s gaze flicked between them. She’d seen that kind of watching too. Kids noticed hunters.\n[L23] Tiffani finally looked at Jhace. “Describe her.”\n[L24] “Dust cloak,” he said. “Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it’s keeping her upright.”\n[L25] Tiffani held that for a beat, then nodded once. “Not mine,” she said. “Not from the glass-house.”\n[L26] “Do you know who she is?” Jhace asked.\n[L27] “I know what she is,” Tiffani said, and her voice went flatter. “A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn’t break.”\n[L28] Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines forming—hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. He let the implication settle where it wanted.\n[L29] Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.\n[L30] He didn’t like that. He also understood it.\n[L31] “That’s three of us,” Jhace said, more to himself than them. “One who holds. One who measures. One who survives.”\n[L32] Chloen’s mouth tightened. “I didn’t—”\n[L33] “I know,” Jhace cut in, not unkind. “You did.”\n[L34] She stopped. Swallowed. That taste was getting old, even for him, and he hadn’t been the one under the slab.\n[L35] Tiffani’s gaze went back to the horizon. “The others will start asking,” she said.\n[L36] “Let them,” Jhace said. His hands flexed once, then stilled on the wedge bar. “But if that slate woman shows up again—”\n[L37] Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate once. Controlled. A marker.\n[L38] “—you tell me,” Jhace finished.\n# 【4†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file4\nMclick Target: \"11:4\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Two. A cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.\r\n[L2] Three. Rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.\r\n[L3] Four. A pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.\r\n[L4] The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.\r\n[L5] When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.\r\n[L6] She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.\r\n[L7] Not loud. Not comforting. Just clear.\r\n[L8] “Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”\r\n[L9] Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.\r\n[L10] Tiffani was a few paces away in the semicircle that had broken into work groups. Slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.\r\n[L11] Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.\r\n[L12] “Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said, and even though Chloen had already watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.\r\n[L13] Someone else answered—one of the important men with rings, maybe, or one of the brace captains. Chloen couldn’t see who. She could hear the shape of the words.\r\n[L14] “Not possible.”\r\n[L15] “It happened,” Jhace said.\r\n[L16] Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.\r\n[L17] Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.\r\n[L18] Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”\r\n[L19] Slate woman.\r\n[L20] Chloen’s skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.\r\n[L21] Chloen didn’t like her. She didn’t know her. She didn’t like the way the woman’s attention had felt like fingers.\r\n[L22] Tiffani’s pencil tapped once. A small sound. A decision sound.\r\n[L23] Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.\r\n[L24] She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.\r\n[L25] The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.\r\n[L26] Her throat tightened.\r\n[L27] She kept it to herself.\r\n[L28] Aunt Lessa most likely dead.\r\n[L29] No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.\r\n[L30] Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else.\r\n[L31] “Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.\r\n[L32] Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.\r\n[L33] “Still here.”\r\n[L34] And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.\r\n[L35] Not crying.\r\n[L36] Not speaking.\r\n[L37] Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.\r\n[L38] Chapter 10 — Mobel (The Shard)\r\n[L39] Mobel didn’t sit in the center of the room.\r\n[L40] Old habit. Old math.\r\n[L41] Center meant eyes. Eyes meant judgment. Judgment meant cages.\n# 【5†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file5\nMclick Target: \"11:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The tremor tried to rise.\n[L2] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\n[L3] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\n[L4] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\n[L5] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\n[L6] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\n[L7] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\n[L8] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\n[L9] His sternum was already too awake.\n[L10] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\n[L11] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\n[L12] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\n[L13] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\n[L14] And now he knew it could be done.\n[L15] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\n[L16] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\n[L17] Not because there was nothing to write. Because there was too much, and the act of recording would change what people thought they were doing. If she raised graphite now, they’d start performing their panic for the page. They’d turn grief into testimony.\n[L18] She wasn’t ready to be that kind of authority.\n[L19] The upper terrace had become a sorting place.\n[L20] Not formally or by decree—by physics.\n[L21] Rubble moved downhill when it could. People moved uphill when they could breathe. Anyone who could still walk drifted toward the wider stone flats where the terraces broadened and the parapets were intact enough to lean on. The broken streets below funneled survivors toward this ledge the way water found low points.\n[L22] Volunteers—if you could call them that—had formed lines. Hands passed stone from hand to hand. Smaller pieces went into baskets. Larger slabs were propped with scavenged timber and wedge bars. Someone had tied a length of rope across a crack in the paving to keep the crowd from stepping onto a section that shifted on the doubled pulse.\n[L23] The work sounded ordinary.\n[L24] Stone scraping. Breath. A cough. A child’s thin crying. A man’s voice calling names into the air until his throat went raw.\n[L25] Ordinary work inside an impossible day.\n[L26] Tiffani moved through it without stepping into anyone’s line. Her eyes tracked loads and fractures the way her hands tracked stars. She counted by seeing.\n[L27] One: a retaining wall seam opened a hair each pulse.\n[L28] Two: a lintel with a new shear line, chalked in red by someone who knew what “don’t stand here” meant.\n[L29] Three: a brace post that had set crooked after liquefaction—compensating now, but only by being loud about it.\n[L30] She was not the only one paying attention. That was the most surprising thing.\n[L31] A woman with a carpenter’s hammer had marked a series of doorframes with wax. A boy with a long pole tapped stones and listened for the hollow note of delamination. Someone—older, with a scarf over their mouth—had turned a smashed cart into a supply table and was rationing water in cups like it mattered, because it did.\n[L32] Tiffani felt the doubled hum in her knee with every step. It registered as a timing problem: the joint wanted one rhythm and the ground insisted on two.\n[L33] She adjusted her stride to the slack phase. It made walking possible.\n[L34] She did not allow herself to enjoy that small mastery.\n# 【6†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file6\nMclick Target: \"11:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] He crouched in front of a man with shaking hands and said something gentle. The man nodded and laughed, the same short laugh as before. Bram laughed back, softer, and helped him stand.\n[L2] “See?” Bram said to no one in particular. “It works.”\n[L3] Chloen looked down at the stone so she didn’t have to look at Bram’s face when he said it.\n[L4] Jhace wasn’t in the bowl anymore.\n[L5] She noticed that absence the way she noticed missing stairs—by almost stepping where something should have been.\n[L6] The emblems on coats and banners had finished settling. Square near the front. Loop nearer the lanes. Drop along the water tables. Fracture pushed outward, closer to the exits.\n[L7] That wasn’t how they’d been.\n[L8] Chloen didn’t know how she knew the difference. She just knew the shapes had moved the way animals moved when you weren’t watching them directly.\n[L9] A brace captain called for hands near the west rib. Chloen drifted with the crowd until she wasn’t near Tiffani anymore, then stopped.\n[L10] No one noticed.\n[L11] That was the trick.\n[L12] She stood where two rope lanes crossed and watched how people flowed around the knot. One lane moved faster. The other slowed and backed up. People grew impatient and leaned, and the lean pushed the timing off by half a beat.\n[L13] Half a beat mattered now.\n[L14] Chloen stepped sideways.\n[L15] Just one pace. Not dramatic. Just enough to stand where the knot wanted to form.\n[L16] The next pull came.\n[L17] People hesitated. The lane loosened. The knot didn’t catch.\n[L18] No one fell.\n[L19] No one noticed her.\n[L20] Chloen’s heart thudded too fast, then slowed when the slack phase came like it was supposed to.\n[L21] She hadn’t meant to do anything.\n[L22] She just didn’t like how the ropes were talking to the crowd.\n[L23] She drifted back toward the stone flats and found the low wall again. Sat. Hugged her elbows. Checked her mouth.\n[L24] Still no metal.\n[L25] That was worse.\n[L26] Tiffani passed close enough that Chloen could hear the pencil scratch. The graphite moved cleanly now, not skirting smears, not hesitating.\n[L27] Chloen wanted to say something. Anything.\n[L28] She didn’t.\n[L29] Across the stadium, Eries moved among the lanes without hurry. People made space for her without being told. She nodded. She listened. She touched shoulders lightly and left them straighter than before.\n[L30] She didn’t look at Chloen.\n[L31] Which meant she knew exactly where Chloen was.\n[L32] The bells rang notice cadence again—short, short, long—and this time Chloen breathed shallow before she remembered she was supposed to.\n[L33] Her body had learned faster than she had.\n[L34] She pressed her thumb into her palm until it hurt, just to make sure pain still worked the same way.\n[L35] It did.\n[L36] Good.\n[L37] A woman nearby whispered, “It’s better already,” and someone else answered, “Thank the Crown,” and a third said, “Thank her,” without needing to name who.\n[L38] Chloen watched the words move through the air like dust settling into cracks.\n[L39] She licked her teeth again.\n[L40] Nothing.\n[L41] She imagined the taste was still there, just buried deeper. Like grit ground into stone so fine you didn’t feel it until later.\n[L42] Chloen slid off the wall and followed the edge of the stadium where shadows cut the lines and the bells sounded farther away.\n[L43] She didn’t know what would happen next.\n[L44] She only knew this:\n[L45] If the world stopped hurting you right away, it didn’t mean it was healed.\n[L46] Sometimes it meant you’d stopped feeling the damage.\n[L47] Chloen kept walking, quiet and uncounted, while the city learned how to behave.\n[L48] Epilogue — Corlexi\n[L49] Corlexi did not return to the floor.\n# 【7†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file7\nMclick Target: \"11:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Then, quieter: “Force applied.”\r\n[L2] Jhace swallowed copper. It scratched.\r\n[L3] Chloen leaned forward, gripping the broken parapet stone with both hands like she could keep herself from falling into the sight of it. Her knuckles were white under dust.\r\n[L4] “Did… did the water—” she started.\r\n[L5] “It turned,” Jhace said. Flat. Confirming. No comfort.\r\n[L6] Chloen blinked hard and looked between the redirected basin and the city that had just not died.\r\n[L7] “How?”\r\n[L8] Jhace didn’t answer because he didn’t have one that wasn’t a lie.\r\n[L9] Tiffani did not look up from her slate. “Not wind,” she said, as if correcting herself before anyone else could. “Not terrain. Too late. Too clean.”\r\n[L10] Jhace’s jaw worked. He tasted metal again.\r\n[L11] He imagined his own hands doing it.\r\n[L12] His sternum tightened like the thought alone tried to pull the line into existence. Heat climbed under his ribs.\r\n[L13] He forced his breath into a measured pull on the slack phase. In. Out.\r\n[L14] He did not move his hands.\r\n[L15] Down in the basin, the diverted wave slammed into rock and scrub and the underside of lower terraces. The impact threw up a fan of mist that glittered with copper-gold flecks even at this distance, catching the kindling-light in flashes that made the air look full of filings.\r\n[L16] Jhace watched the mist drift and settle and watched, for one heartbeat, a section of spray hang wrong—suspended, slow—before gravity reclaimed it.\r\n[L17] Field residue.\r\n[L18] A use.\r\n[L19] A cost.\r\n[L20] Somewhere down there, someone was paying.\r\n[L21] Jhace turned his head enough to look at Tiffani’s slate at last—not to read it, but to see where she was looking.\r\n[L22] Her pencil stopped for a single beat.\r\n[L23] She wrote one new line, crisp as a stamped mark:\r\n[L24] M3: Mass-water vector deviation. Subject unknown.\r\n[L25] Then she added, smaller:\r\n[L26] Applied at last-second proximity.\r\n[L27] Jhace stared at the words and felt his hunger sharpen into direction.\r\n[L28] Somebody near the waterline. Somebody close enough to get drowned if they missed. Somebody who had touched the ocean and lived.\r\n[L29] He looked back down the face, scanning for a human scale point in the chaos. Too far. Too much spray. Too many moving pieces.\r\n[L30] Chloen’s voice was small. “Is it… like what you did?”\r\n[L31] Jhace’s hands flexed once, involuntary. The tremor tried to rise.\r\n[L32] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\r\n[L33] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\r\n[L34] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\r\n[L35] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\r\n[L36] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\r\n[L37] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\r\n[L38] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\r\n[L39] His sternum was already too awake.\r\n[L40] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\r\n[L41] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\r\n[L42] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\r\n[L43] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\r\n[L44] And now he knew it could be done.\r\n[L45] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\r\n[L46] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\r\n[L47] Not because there was nothing to write.\n# 【8†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file8\nMclick Target: \"11:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Bram didn’t clap, but his shoulders loosened like he’d set a pack down.\r\n[L2] Tiffani finally wrote.\r\n[L3] Just one line.\r\n[L4] Chloen couldn’t see the words, but she saw the graphite move around a darker smear already on the slate, like water avoiding oil.\r\n[L5] Jhace turned away.\r\n[L6] Not far. Just enough.\r\n[L7] Like he was done listening.\r\n[L8] Chloen stared at Eries and tried to figure out what felt wrong.\r\n[L9] It wasn’t the words. The words made sense.\r\n[L10] It was the way they fit too easily. Like a puzzle with pieces shaved down so they’d stop resisting.\r\n[L11] Chloen looked at the emblems stitched into coats and pinned to collars across the crowd. Square. Drop. Loop. Fracture.\r\n[L12] They weren’t where they’d been before.\r\n[L13] She didn’t know how she knew that.\r\n[L14] She just did.\r\n[L15] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\r\n[L16] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\r\n[L17] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\r\n[L18] Tiffani didn’t look down.\r\n[L19] But her pencil stopped.\r\n[L20] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\r\n[L21] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\r\n[L22] Camera (baseline): The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\r\n[L23] Sensory intrusion: The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear. Timing problem. Two beats where there should have been one.\r\n[L24] Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.\r\n[L25] She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.\r\n[L26] Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.\r\n[L27] People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.\r\n[L28] That was new.\r\n[L29] That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.\r\n[L30] Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.\r\n[L31] He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.\r\n[L32] Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.\r\n[L33] Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.\r\n[L34] The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.\r\n[L35] Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day.\n# 【9†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn11file9\nMclick Target: \"11:9\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The lintel fell harmlessly because the people under it had cleared in that half-breath.\r\n[L2] Tiffani didn’t let it become myth. She let it become a list.\r\n[L3] M1: Jhace — slab suspension — tremor + copper surge + sternum lock.\r\n[L4] M2: unknown — lintel arrest — collapse.\r\n[L5] Space left for more.\r\n[L6] Across the sky, the orbital band brightened as the sun angle shifted, making the debris flash like a scar being shown off.\r\n[L7] Tiffani stopped long enough to look at the Rift again: black in blue, unchanged.\r\n[L8] She hummed once, steady, and wrote the last line on that slate page:\r\n[L9] New baseline established.\r\n[L10] Then she turned away from the horizon and back toward the city’s breaking points, because whatever the sky had become, people were still made of stone and water and breath—and those were failing in ways she could, at least sometimes, predict.\r\n[L11] Chapter 4 — Jhace (Firebreak)\r\n[L12] Jhace kept moving because stopping made the shaking worse.\r\n[L13] Not always. Not forever. Right now.\r\n[L14] The bell cadence pulled him through the quarter like a hook in the ribs—fast peals from Brace Street, then the staggered answering ring from a lane he knew by smell more than signage. The kit strap sawed at his shoulder. His hands wouldn’t hold still on the wedge bar, so he let them hang and used his legs.\r\n[L15] Copper still sat on his tongue. Every swallow scraped.\r\n[L16] The doubled hum underfoot stayed. It wasn’t an aftershock. It was a new layer to the world, pressed through stone, through pipe, through bone. The city’s old pipe-thrum was still there, but it had company now, and the company didn’t know how to behave.\r\n[L17] People ran in bursts—start, freeze, start again—like the ground kept asking their bodies a question they couldn’t answer. A man with a basket slammed into Jhace’s shoulder and spun away without apologizing. A woman hauled a child by the sleeve, the kid’s feet skidding, eyes wide.\r\n[L18] Jhace pointed once, short and sharp. “Open ground. Away from façades.”\r\n[L19] Some listened. Most didn’t. Bells made people think there was one safe direction. Bells were a lie on a day like this.\r\n[L20] He cut left at the corner by the tannery vents—hot, sour air—and almost ran into a knot of bodies jammed in the street. Not staring at the horizon now. Staring at something immediate.\r\n[L21] Fire.\r\n[L22] A building at the far end of the lane had lit up like a lantern. Two windows vomiting orange, smoke rolling out under the eaves in thick, oily layers. The flames weren’t tall; they were hungry and spreading sideways along old wood and pitch. Heat shimmered above the door like glass trying to become water.\r\n[L23] The lane was a choke point: stone walls tight, terraces stacked, one way out blocked by that burning front.\r\n[L24] People were pressed against each other, coughing, trying to turn around in a space that didn’t have room. Some were trying to push through anyway, as if speed could argue with flame.\r\n[L25] Jhace’s brain stopped seeing “fire” and started seeing constraints.\r\n[L26] Narrow lane. Wind pulling toward the Exit Face in pulses. Smoke already leaning. Limited exits. Water sources? Pipe mains? Steam vents?\r\n[L27] He ran.\r\n[L28] Not into the crowd—into the edges, where he could move. He shoved through a gap, shoulder checking a man who was frozen mid-step. “Back,” he snapped. “Back from the door. You’ll feed it.”\r\n[L29] A woman screamed at him, words useless. He didn’t answer.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",

        "ba535c06-4c18-4672-a738-3973c1ddf277"

          "content_type": "tether_browsing_display",
          "result": "# 【0†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file0\nMclick Target: \"6:0\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Jhace held his hands out and kept sweeping, smaller motions now, compressing and directing. He wasn’t blowing the fire out. He was smothering it, stripping it of oxygen and drowning it in wet heat.\r\n[L2] His sternum locked. Breath cut. Vision narrowed.\r\n[L3] Copper flooded his mouth.\r\n[L4] He forced the motion anyway. Tiny adjustments. Keep the steam sheet low. Keep it in the door. Don’t let it backwash onto the crowd.\r\n[L5] The fire fought once—hungry, bright—then choked again as the steam packed the doorway.\r\n[L6] The orange dwindled to a pulse behind wet smoke.\r\n[L7] Jhace’s arms trembled from shoulder to fingertips. Not effort like lifting a hammer—effort like holding a vibration in place while your bones tried to resonate with it.\r\n[L8] His lungs finally dragged in a thin breath. It tasted like copper and wet ash.\r\n[L9] He let the wind go on the slack phase.\r\n[L10] The steam thinned. The smoke changed—less black, more gray. The doorway still radiated heat, but it wasn’t spitting flame anymore.\r\n[L11] Jhace staggered one step back and braced a hand on the stone wall because his knees had decided they were optional.\r\n[L12] He looked at his hands.\r\n[L13] They were shaking again, worse than before—tiny jerks, out of sync, as if his nerves were trying to find the doubled harmonic and missing.\r\n[L14] A cough tore out of him. Metal taste surged. He spat into the gutter without thinking and hated himself for it immediately.\r\n[L15] A woman pushed forward from the crowd, eyes wild. “Is it—?”\r\n[L16] “Not out,” Jhace said. “But it’s not eating the street. Get them away from the door. Now.”\r\n[L17] She stared at him like he’d grown new bones. Then she turned and started shouting directions, and some people listened because a loud voice in a crisis becomes law.\r\n[L18] Jhace wiped his mouth with his sleeve and swallowed the rest of the copper anyway.\r\n[L19] He leaned close to the doorway’s edge where the steam had cooled the stone enough to touch without skin sticking.\r\n[L20] He didn’t go in yet.\r\n[L21] He listened.\r\n[L22] Through the wet hiss and the bell cadence and the planet’s doubled hum, he heard something inside the building that wasn’t fire.\r\n[L23] A cough. Human. Close.\r\n[L24] Jhace set his jaw.\r\n[L25] He raised his hands again—smaller this time—and shaped the air to hold the smoke back from the doorway like a curtain.\r\n[L26] His sternum burned.\r\n[L27] He did it anyway.\r\n[L28] Chapter 5 — Chloen (The Child Under the Slab)\r\n[L29] Chloen didn’t know the bell codes.\r\n[L30] She only knew bells meant grown-ups stopped pretending.\r\n[L31] It started like a feeling in her teeth.\r\n[L32] Not pain. A buzzing, like when you put your ear too close to the pipe runs in the Underface and the whole world sounds like it’s inside a shell. She was on the mid-terrace steps with a bundle of rags under one arm because Aunt Lessa had sent her down to fetch clean wrap from the stitch shop, and Chloen had tried to walk like she wasn’t late.\r\n[L33] Then the air got thin.\r\n[L34] Not cold. Thin.\r\n[L35] Her ears popped so hard it made her blink, and when she tried to take a bigger breath to fix it, her chest didn’t fill right—like someone had pinched the air.\r\n[L36] Smoke did something wrong above the rooftops. It didn’t rise. It pulled sideways and down, sucked back into chimneys as if the houses were drinking it.\r\n[L37] She stopped on the step and watched it, because her brain didn’t know what else to do.\r\n[L38] People in the street stopped too.\r\n[L39] Not all at once.\n# 【1†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file1\nMclick Target: \"6:1\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The particulate glittered in it, catching lamplight and daylight in different ways at once.\r\n[L2] A slab of stone lay broken in the street. Fresh fracture faces shone pale. People were gathered around it, some helping, some staring, some arguing.\r\n[L3] Her eyes went immediately to the failure pattern.\r\n[L4] She traced the break line up with her gaze: façade shear, mortar flow point, post-bend, then fracture. It matched her earlier forecast. Liquefaction, set wrong, then catastrophic shear under its own weight.\r\n[L5] She wrote:\r\n[L6] Collapse: delayed shear following re-solidification. Failure initiated at mortar seam—flow residue visible.\r\n[L7] Then she looked for the secondary phenomenon.\r\n[L8] A man was on one elbow near the edge of the rubble. Dust in his hair. Kit strap across his chest. His hands were held out in front of him as if he didn’t trust them to behave. They were shaking. Not weak tremor. A full-body signal localized in the hands like the nerves didn’t know where to settle.\r\n[L9] His mouth was tight, jaw working. He swallowed once, hard.\r\n[L10] She saw the copper stain at the corner of his lips.\r\n[L11] He had been the brace.\r\n[L12] Tiffani stopped at a distance that did not crowd him and crouched, not for kindness but to get her eyes level with his hands.\r\n[L13] The tremor had a rhythm.\r\n[L14] It pulsed in sync with the doubled harmonic underfoot, as if his body was still trying to match the new baseline and failing.\r\n[L15] She held her own breath still and listened with her skin. The air around him prickled. The copper-gold grit on her slate edge stood slightly proud, lifting on static.\r\n[L16] She wrote without looking away from his hands:\r\n[L17] Subject: post-event tremor synchronized to doubled harmonic. Particulate attraction increased near subject.\r\n[L18] A woman a few steps away was already writing too—chalk on slate, numbers in a column, counting under her breath like it was prayer or punishment.\r\n[L19] Tiffani noted it.\r\n[L20] Secondary observer: recording intervals. Fixation: subject’s sternum/hand tremor.\r\n[L21] Above the rooftops, the Rift was visible—black in blue, stars in day—still open, still wrong.\r\n[L22] On the horizon the Exit Face stood like a new continent turned vertical. Dust boiled at its base. The curvature at the edges was clearer now that her brain accepted the scale. It was not a wall. It was a rim.\r\n[L23] Her eyes flicked upward.\r\n[L24] A faint band had begun to form across the sky. Not cloud. Not smoke. A thin arc that caught light differently, shifting brightness as it moved.\r\n[L25] The ring.\r\n[L26] She wrote:\r\n[L27] Orbital debris band visible. Arc across dome. Brightness varies with angle.\r\n[L28] The man’s name cut through dust. “Jhace!”\r\n[L29] He tried to stand and failed on the first attempt, his hands curling involuntarily as if they were gripping an invisible bar.\r\n[L30] Tiffani watched his sternum. His breathing pattern was wrong—short pulls, clipped, like his ribs didn’t want to expand.\r\n[L31] “What did it feel like?” she asked, voice pitched low so it wouldn’t carry into the crowd.\r\n[L32] He looked at her as if she’d asked him to describe a bolt with feelings.\r\n[L33] Then he swallowed again. Copper.\r\n[L34] “Like—” he started, then stopped.\r\n[L35] His hand shook harder for two pulses.\r\n[L36] “Like a line,” he finished. “Tied through me.”\r\n[L37] Tiffani nodded once and wrote:\r\n[L38] Phenomenology: “line tied through sternum” during suspension. Breath restriction. Copper taste surge.\r\n[L39] A person behind her retched and spat into the gutter, the spit dark with metallic flecks.\n# 【2†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file2\nMclick Target: \"6:2\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The lintel fell harmlessly because the people under it had cleared in that half-breath.\r\n[L2] Tiffani didn’t let it become myth. She let it become a list.\r\n[L3] M1: Jhace — slab suspension — tremor + copper surge + sternum lock.\r\n[L4] M2: unknown — lintel arrest — collapse.\r\n[L5] Space left for more.\r\n[L6] Across the sky, the orbital band brightened as the sun angle shifted, making the debris flash like a scar being shown off.\r\n[L7] Tiffani stopped long enough to look at the Rift again: black in blue, unchanged.\r\n[L8] She hummed once, steady, and wrote the last line on that slate page:\r\n[L9] New baseline established.\r\n[L10] Then she turned away from the horizon and back toward the city’s breaking points, because whatever the sky had become, people were still made of stone and water and breath—and those were failing in ways she could, at least sometimes, predict.\r\n[L11] Chapter 4 — Jhace (Firebreak)\r\n[L12] Jhace kept moving because stopping made the shaking worse.\r\n[L13] Not always. Not forever. Right now.\r\n[L14] The bell cadence pulled him through the quarter like a hook in the ribs—fast peals from Brace Street, then the staggered answering ring from a lane he knew by smell more than signage. The kit strap sawed at his shoulder. His hands wouldn’t hold still on the wedge bar, so he let them hang and used his legs.\r\n[L15] Copper still sat on his tongue. Every swallow scraped.\r\n[L16] The doubled hum underfoot stayed. It wasn’t an aftershock. It was a new layer to the world, pressed through stone, through pipe, through bone. The city’s old pipe-thrum was still there, but it had company now, and the company didn’t know how to behave.\r\n[L17] People ran in bursts—start, freeze, start again—like the ground kept asking their bodies a question they couldn’t answer. A man with a basket slammed into Jhace’s shoulder and spun away without apologizing. A woman hauled a child by the sleeve, the kid’s feet skidding, eyes wide.\r\n[L18] Jhace pointed once, short and sharp. “Open ground. Away from façades.”\r\n[L19] Some listened. Most didn’t. Bells made people think there was one safe direction. Bells were a lie on a day like this.\r\n[L20] He cut left at the corner by the tannery vents—hot, sour air—and almost ran into a knot of bodies jammed in the street. Not staring at the horizon now. Staring at something immediate.\r\n[L21] Fire.\r\n[L22] A building at the far end of the lane had lit up like a lantern. Two windows vomiting orange, smoke rolling out under the eaves in thick, oily layers. The flames weren’t tall; they were hungry and spreading sideways along old wood and pitch. Heat shimmered above the door like glass trying to become water.\r\n[L23] The lane was a choke point: stone walls tight, terraces stacked, one way out blocked by that burning front.\r\n[L24] People were pressed against each other, coughing, trying to turn around in a space that didn’t have room. Some were trying to push through anyway, as if speed could argue with flame.\r\n[L25] Jhace’s brain stopped seeing “fire” and started seeing constraints.\r\n[L26] Narrow lane. Wind pulling toward the Exit Face in pulses. Smoke already leaning. Limited exits. Water sources? Pipe mains? Steam vents?\r\n[L27] He ran.\r\n[L28] Not into the crowd—into the edges, where he could move. He shoved through a gap, shoulder checking a man who was frozen mid-step. “Back,” he snapped. “Back from the door. You’ll feed it.”\r\n[L29] A woman screamed at him, words useless. He didn’t answer.\n# 【3†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file3\nMclick Target: \"6:3\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Dust lifted from the floor in a fine, lazy drift as if gravity had loosened.\r\n[L2] Jhace’s first thought was structural. A brace slip. A terrace shift. A pipe main failing somewhere it shouldn’t.\r\n[L3] Then the bell rang.\r\n[L4] Not the forge bell. Not the shift bell. The emergency bell, from above, from the streets.\r\n[L5] One strike. Uneven.\r\n[L6] Another. A different direction.\r\n[L7] He didn’t stand there and decide. He moved. Chalk down, slate left open on the bench, hands already reaching for his kit by habit—wedge bar, line, quick clamps, a coil of cord, collar keys. Moving. Then thinking.\r\n[L8] The doubled hum thickened, like something was taking a breath through stone.\r\n[L9] He hooked the kit strap over his shoulder, scooped the keys, and went for the ladder shaft.\r\n[L10] More bells started.\r\n[L11] Different streets. Same urgency.\r\n[L12] The Underface corridor was tight, stone on both sides, pipework overhead sweating. People were already moving, faces pale in the lamplight, some running, some walking too carefully like they’d forgotten how to trust the floor. The air tasted dusty, and with every step he could feel the vibration climb from the soles of his boots up through his legs.\r\n[L13] He took the ladder two steps at a time. The rungs trembled under his hands.\r\n[L14] Halfway up, the pressure changed. It wasn’t wind. It was absence. Like the air had been thinned with a knife.\r\n[L15] His ears popped hard. His chest tightened as if someone had set a strap around his ribs and pulled.\r\n[L16] He tried to inhale and got less than he asked for.\r\n[L17] Below, somewhere in the Underface, a torch flame bent sideways. Smoke that should have risen got pulled flat.\r\n[L18] Jhace swallowed and forced a breath through it. He didn’t stop. Stopping didn’t help.\r\n[L19] He came up through a service hatch behind a row of terraces into light that looked normal until his eyes adjusted and he realized normal was the insult. Blue kindling-light sky. People on the street. Shop signs. Dust in the air.\r\n[L20] And a deep, wrong hum running through all of it, doubling everything.\r\n[L21] The street had gone still in a way that wasn’t calm. Tools lay where they’d dropped. A cart sat half-hitched, mule wide-eyed, nostrils flared, fighting the harness. Faces were turned in one direction as if there was only one thing in the world worth seeing.\r\n[L22] Jhace stepped out into the street and followed their gaze.\r\n[L23] On the plain beyond Saint Merro, something had risen.\r\n[L24] At first his mind tried to name it small. Wall. Cliff. A terrace collapse out beyond the far districts. Then the scale hit and the name fell apart.\r\n[L25] It wasn’t a wall. It was the edge of the world being forced up.\r\n[L26] Banded strata—light over dark, dark over light—turned vertical like pages ripped from a book and held upright. The thing curved away left and right until it vanished into its own distance. Its base was lost in a brown-gray cloud that boiled without flame.\r\n[L27] Above it, the sky was wounded.\r\n[L28] A hole—no, a cut. A clean absence where blue stopped and black began. Stars inside it in full day. Not faint. Hard points of light like they’d been drilled through the air.\r\n[L29] The edge shimmered, not like heat, like the air couldn’t decide where to exist.\r\n[L30] People made sounds behind him, but the air was thin enough that sound didn’t carry right. It felt muffled, like cotton in the world.\r\n[L31] Jhace stood with his kit strap biting his shoulder and tried to make his eyes tell him something usable.\n# 【4†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file4\nMclick Target: \"6:4\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] You are a person. But it helps me remember.\"\r\n[L2] Tiffani tracked the accuracy: coarse, but usable.\r\n[L3] Jhace watched the street edges. Load paths. Crack marks. Rope lines. He moved like he was always half a step away from catching something.\r\n[L4] Chloen watched Bram instead. Not the words. The hands. He kept them open. Visible.\r\n[L5] They reached the last ramp down to the stadium bowl. The crowd density increased. Rope lines became lanes. Guard posts multiplied. Someone had hung glass horns on a scaffold to carry a speaker's voice.\r\n[L6] Bram slowed and pointed with two fingers.\r\n[L7] \"Stay together,\" he said. Then, as if he couldn't stop himself, \"Unless you want to try the thrilling sport of getting lost in a disaster crowd. It's like hide-and-seek except the seeker is bureaucracy.\"\r\n[L8] Jhace's eyes cut toward Chloen.\r\n[L9] \"Where'd you come from anyway?\" Bram asked, still in that half-joking tone. \"Where's your mum? Your guardian?\"\r\n[L10] Chloen went still.\r\n[L11] Tiffani felt the change before she saw it. The air around the girl tightened. The grit on her slate edge lifted and did not settle on the first pulse.\r\n[L12] Bram's grin faltered as the question landed and failed to be a joke.\r\n[L13] Chapter 12 - Chloen / Bram (The Question)\r\n[L14] Chloen could still feel the word guardian like a thumb pressed into a bruise.\r\n[L15] It was not the first time someone had asked.\r\n[L16] Usually it was a neighbor. A shopkeeper. A teacher who wanted a signature.\r\n[L17] Today it was a guard with a joke face.\r\n[L18] She didn't look at Bram. Looking at people made them real, and real people asked follow-ups.\r\n[L19] She looked at his boots instead. Leather cracked at the toe. Ash in the seams. A smear of blue paint on the left heel.\r\n[L20] She tried to answer in the smallest way.\r\n[L21] \"Lost,\" she said.\r\n[L22] The word came out flat. Not crying. Not lying. Not telling.\r\n[L23] Bram blinked. The grin fell off him like it had been hooked on wrong.\r\n[L24] \"Ah,\" he said, and the joke engine in him revved and stalled. \"Right. Sorry. That was... I stepped in it. Like, literally. Like a horse in a market. Except there are no horses. You get it.\" He stopped. \"You don't have to get it.\"\r\n[L25] Jhace made a sound through his nose that might have been a laugh if it had been any other day.\r\n[L26] \"Finish it,\" Jhace said.\r\n[L27] Bram stared at him. \"What?\"\r\n[L28] \"The horse thing,\" Jhace said. \"If you start a stupid metaphor you don't get to leave it bleeding in the street.\"\r\n[L29] Tiffani's jaw tightened. She looked past all of them toward the rope lanes and the scaffold horns.\r\n[L30] Bram opened his mouth, realized the rescue attempt would only make it worse, and tried anyway.\r\n[L31] \"Fine,\" he said. \"I stepped in it like a horse in a market, except the market is a disaster and the horse is me and the...\" He waved a hand. \"The point is: I'm sorry.\"\r\n[L32] Chloen's mouth twitched. She hated that it did. She kept her face still. But a small breath escaped her nose.\r\n[L33] Bram saw it and looked relieved like he'd managed to not make things worse by accident. He had. Barely.\r\n[L34] \"Lost,\" Jhace repeated, softer than his voice had been in the forge. Not gentle. Just acknowledging.\r\n[L35] Chloen nodded once. She did not add aunt.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",

          "tether_id": null
        },
