        "content": {
          "assets": null,
          "content_type": "tether_browsing_display",
          "result": "# 【0†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file0\nMclick Target: \"22:0\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\n[L2] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\n[L3] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\n[L4] Tiffani didn’t look down.\n[L5] But her pencil stopped.\n[L6] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\n[L7] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\n[L8] The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\n[L9] The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear—a timing problem: two beats where there should have been one.\n[L10] Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.\n[L11] She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.\n[L12] Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.\n[L13] People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.\n[L14] That was new.\n[L15] That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.\n[L16] Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.\n[L17] He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.\n[L18] Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.\n[L19] Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.\n[L20] The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.\n[L21] Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day. His posture read administrative fatigue as if it were a uniform requirement.\n[L22] Eries stood in the center.\n[L23] High class by cut and fit alone—no dust-stained hem, no patched seams, no improvised wrap. She wore a mantle that could have been ceremonial if it hadn’t been practical wool. That was the trick. She looked like she belonged and like she’d come to help.\n[L24] She didn’t raise her hands for silence. She waited until the bell cadence finished and let the last long note die.\n[L25] The stadium quieted on its own.\n[L26] Tiffani catalogued the quiet like a structural event.\n[L27] Eries spoke without amplification and still carried.\n[L28] “Your air is changing,” she said, and didn’t dress it in myth. “It will keep changing. The pull and slack will continue until the planet settles into a new baseline.”\n[L29] Baseline. Useful word. Correct enough to calm.\n# 【1†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file1\nMclick Target: \"22:1\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\n[L2] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\n[L3] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\n[L4] Tiffani didn’t look down.\n[L5] But her pencil stopped.\n[L6] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\n[L7] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\n[L8] The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\n[L9] The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear—a timing problem: two beats where there should have been one.\n[L10] Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.\n[L11] She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.\n[L12] Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.\n[L13] People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.\n[L14] That was new.\n[L15] That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.\n[L16] Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.\n[L17] He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.\n[L18] Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.\n[L19] Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.\n[L20] The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.\n[L21] Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day. His posture read administrative fatigue as if it were a uniform requirement.\n[L22] Eries stood in the center.\n[L23] High class by cut and fit alone—no dust-stained hem, no patched seams, no improvised wrap. She wore a mantle that could have been ceremonial if it hadn’t been practical wool. That was the trick. She looked like she belonged and like she’d come to help.\n[L24] She didn’t raise her hands for silence. She waited until the bell cadence finished and let the last long note die.\n[L25] The stadium quieted on its own.\n[L26] Tiffani catalogued the quiet like a structural event.\n[L27] Eries spoke without amplification and still carried.\n[L28] “Your air is changing,” she said, and didn’t dress it in myth. “It will keep changing. The pull and slack will continue until the planet settles into a new baseline.”\n[L29] Baseline. Useful word. Correct enough to calm.\n# 【2†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file2\nMclick Target: \"22:2\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Not because the blood was not data.\n[L2] Because the act of writing had just become part of the event.\n[L3] She could feel the graphite under her fingers like a conductor.\n[L4] She stood and shifted one step to spare her knee.\n[L5] She did not look at Bram.\n[L6] She did not look at Jhace.\n[L7] She looked at the horn-glass amplifiers and the copper wire bracing and the way the wire shivered on the baseline as if it was listening.\n[L8] Then she exhaled on the slack phase and moved toward the aisle line where the blood had been, because measurement required proximity and proximity now carried cost.\n[L9] Chapter 15 – Tiffani (The Omission)\n[L10] The bells rang the Assembly cadence.\n[L11] Not alarm, evacuation, or mourning.\n[L12] Instruction.\n[L13] Three low. One high. Pause. Then the repeat—slightly off, like everything else now. The harmonic bled into the bronze, stretched the spacing between notes until the rhythm felt learned rather than remembered.\n[L14] Tiffani stood where she could see the whole of it without being seen herself.\n[L15] The crowd had been arranged for sound, not comfort. That was deliberate. Stone risers angled shallow toward the dais. Rope lines kept bodies from pressing too close to the glasswork that carried the amplification. Brace captains flanked the aisles, hands loose, eyes working.\n[L16] People had stopped talking before the bells finished.\n[L17] That mattered.\n[L18] They were waiting to be told what the Event was.\n[L19] Corlexi stood already at the center of the platform when the last echo died. No procession. No flourish. He wore Crown gray—clean lines, no ornament—and the way he held himself said he had practiced stillness until it read as calm instead of fear.\n[L20] Tiffani opened her slate.\n[L21] Not to write yet. To feel the grit along the edge lift and settle. Baseline check. It answered.\n[L22] Corlexi began.\n[L23] “Citizens of Saint Merro and the upper terraces,” he said, voice carried clean through the glass horns. “We are convened under conditions without precedent.”\n[L24] That was true. Also useless.\n[L25] “We have experienced a planetary structural failure event,” he continued. “External in origin. Sudden. Unpreventable.”\n[L26] External. Tiffani underlined it in her mind and waited for the evidence that would follow.\n[L27] “It has compromised the grid,” Corlexi said. “Altered pressure dynamics. Introduced sustained harmonic instability.”\n[L28] He was careful with verbs. Passive constructions. No actor named.\n[L29] The crowd stayed quiet. Too quiet. People leaned forward a fraction with each pulse, then settled back on the slack. They had learned the rhythm faster than she’d expected.\n[L30] Corlexi spoke of logistics.\n[L31] Assembly points. Rationing. Repair priorities. The need for order while assessment continued. He named the glass-houses—plural—and acknowledged the Underface without promising aid he couldn’t deliver.\n[L32] All of it was competent.\n[L33] All of it was insufficient.\n[L34] Tiffani waited for him to say how big.\n[L35] She waited for where else.\n[L36] She waited for him to acknowledge the hole that ran through the planet like a throat.\n[L37] He did not.\n[L38] Around her, reactions registered in micro-movements.\n[L39] Relief, where people heard “external” and translated it to not our fault.\n[L40] Anxiety, where “assessment ongoing” sounded like no answers yet.\n[L41] Bram stood two rows down, assigned to the aisle. He leaned on his spear like it was a prop, jaw tight, eyes bright with the effort of paying attention. He nodded at the right places. When Corlexi spoke about “citizen cooperation,” Bram’s shoulders eased, just a touch.\n[L42] Jhace did not nod.\n[L43] He stood back from the risers, weight on his heels, arms folded like he was bracing a wall.\n# 【3†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file3\nMclick Target: \"22:3\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Not because the blood was not data.\n[L2] Because the act of writing had just become part of the event.\n[L3] She could feel the graphite under her fingers like a conductor.\n[L4] She stood and shifted one step to spare her knee.\n[L5] She did not look at Bram.\n[L6] She did not look at Jhace.\n[L7] She looked at the horn-glass amplifiers and the copper wire bracing and the way the wire shivered on the baseline as if it was listening.\n[L8] Then she exhaled on the slack phase and moved toward the aisle line where the blood had been, because measurement required proximity and proximity now carried cost.\n[L9] Chapter 15 – Tiffani (The Omission)\n[L10] The bells rang the Assembly cadence.\n[L11] Not alarm, evacuation, or mourning.\n[L12] Instruction.\n[L13] Three low. One high. Pause. Then the repeat—slightly off, like everything else now. The harmonic bled into the bronze, stretched the spacing between notes until the rhythm felt learned rather than remembered.\n[L14] Tiffani stood where she could see the whole of it without being seen herself.\n[L15] The crowd had been arranged for sound, not comfort. That was deliberate. Stone risers angled shallow toward the dais. Rope lines kept bodies from pressing too close to the glasswork that carried the amplification. Brace captains flanked the aisles, hands loose, eyes working.\n[L16] People had stopped talking before the bells finished.\n[L17] That mattered.\n[L18] They were waiting to be told what the Event was.\n[L19] Corlexi stood already at the center of the platform when the last echo died. No procession. No flourish. He wore Crown gray—clean lines, no ornament—and the way he held himself said he had practiced stillness until it read as calm instead of fear.\n[L20] Tiffani opened her slate.\n[L21] Not to write yet. To feel the grit along the edge lift and settle. Baseline check. It answered.\n[L22] Corlexi began.\n[L23] “Citizens of Saint Merro and the upper terraces,” he said, voice carried clean through the glass horns. “We are convened under conditions without precedent.”\n[L24] That was true. Also useless.\n[L25] “We have experienced a planetary structural failure event,” he continued. “External in origin. Sudden. Unpreventable.”\n[L26] External. Tiffani underlined it in her mind and waited for the evidence that would follow.\n[L27] “It has compromised the grid,” Corlexi said. “Altered pressure dynamics. Introduced sustained harmonic instability.”\n[L28] He was careful with verbs. Passive constructions. No actor named.\n[L29] The crowd stayed quiet. Too quiet. People leaned forward a fraction with each pulse, then settled back on the slack. They had learned the rhythm faster than she’d expected.\n[L30] Corlexi spoke of logistics.\n[L31] Assembly points. Rationing. Repair priorities. The need for order while assessment continued. He named the glass-houses—plural—and acknowledged the Underface without promising aid he couldn’t deliver.\n[L32] All of it was competent.\n[L33] All of it was insufficient.\n[L34] Tiffani waited for him to say how big.\n[L35] She waited for where else.\n[L36] She waited for him to acknowledge the hole that ran through the planet like a throat.\n[L37] He did not.\n[L38] Around her, reactions registered in micro-movements.\n[L39] Relief, where people heard “external” and translated it to not our fault.\n[L40] Anxiety, where “assessment ongoing” sounded like no answers yet.\n[L41] Bram stood two rows down, assigned to the aisle. He leaned on his spear like it was a prop, jaw tight, eyes bright with the effort of paying attention. He nodded at the right places. When Corlexi spoke about “citizen cooperation,” Bram’s shoulders eased, just a touch.\n[L42] Jhace did not nod.\n[L43] He stood back from the risers, weight on his heels, arms folded like he was bracing a wall.\n# 【4†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file4\nMclick Target: \"22:4\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Love a man who brings his own teeth to the problem.\"\n[L2] Jhace's mouth tightened. Not a smile. Not not.\n[L3] The guard's grin widened as if that was permission.\n[L4] \"Name's Bram. Interning with the Crown. Which is a polite way of saying they put me where the stone falls and hope I learn fast.\"\n[L5] He paused, then added, \"I did not choose the phrase 'interning with the Crown.' The phrase chose me.\"\n[L6] Tiffani felt her knee register load and adjusted her stride. Bram's voice was noise in the measurement.\n[L7] Jhace looked at Bram's sash. \"Intern,\" he repeated.\n[L8] \"Yes.\" Bram nodded too hard. \"Like a nail that isn't sure if it's headed for wood or thumb.\"\n[L9] He started to wave them forward, then stopped mid-gesture like he could hear his own metaphor.\n[L10] \"That one was bad,\" Bram said. \"Forget I said thumb. The Crown would prefer no thumbs were mentioned.\"\n[L11] Jhace's eyes narrowed.\n[L12] \"Finish it,\" he said.\n[L13] Bram blinked. \"What?\"\n[L14] \"The joke,\" Jhace said. \"You started it. You finish it.\"\n[L15] Bram's mouth opened, closed, opened again. \"It's not a joke. It's a warning. A poorly hammered warning.\"\n[L16] \"Still,\" Jhace said.\n[L17] Tiffani looked away from both of them. The doubled hum under the street held steady. The air pulsed thin-thick. This mattered more than whatever Bram was doing with words.\n[L18] Bram exhaled. \"Fine. Intern is like a nail. If I go in straight, I hold. If I go in crooked, I split the work and everyone blames the hammer.\"\n[L19] He made a face like he regretted all of it.\n[L20] Jhace nodded once, satisfied in a way that made no sense.\n[L21] Chloen made a small sound. Not quite a laugh. It still existed in her chest and surprised her when it came out.\n[L22] Tiffani's pencil hand twitched. She did not write \"Bram: humor coping mechanism\" even though the line wanted to exist.\n[L23] Bram waved them into the moving column. \"You three with me. Small group. Easier to keep alive.\"\n[L24] \"You're escorting,\" Tiffani said.\n[L25] \"That's the word.\" Bram glanced down at his sash like it might correct him. \"Escort. Guide. Herd. Herd is wrong. People are not goats.\" He paused. \"Most people are not goats.\"\n[L26] Jhace made a sound through his nose.\n[L27] Tiffani kept walking.\n[L28] They moved through a corridor of terraces toward the basin flats. The stadium sat below, a broad bowl carved into the stone, its seating rings cut for sound carry. The Crown used it for proclamations and festival trials. Today it was a loud throat for logistics.\n[L29] On the way, they passed a notice board with fresh chalk marks under the posted sheet: arrows, times, assembly colors.\n[L30] A second bell station rang in the same cadence. The pattern held.\n[L31] Bram kept talking like silence was a fault.\n[L32] \"If anyone feels like they're going to pass out on the inhale, you lean into the slack phase and pretend you're a bellows. In. Out. Like you're feeding a forge. Which is, yes, an awful comparison because you are not a forge. You are a person. But it helps me remember.\"\n[L33] Tiffani tracked the accuracy: coarse, but usable.\n[L34] Jhace watched the street edges. Load paths. Crack marks. Rope lines. He moved like he was always half a step away from catching something.\n# 【5†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file5\nMclick Target: \"22:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Ruin was honest. Ruin paid you back quickly. Ruin didn’t pretend to forgive.\r\n[L2] Purity had taken him in prison because Purity offered something ruin never did: a line you could walk and not hate yourself every step.\r\n[L3] He didn’t pretend Purity made him good. Purity made him intentional.\r\n[L4] “I don’t know,” Mobel said. “Not yet.”\r\n[L5] Kerrin’s gaze stayed steady. “You moved a wall of water.”\r\n[L6] Mobel’s throat worked. “I redirected it.”\r\n[L7] “Still.”\r\n[L8] Mobel closed his eyes for half a breath and felt the phantom of it again—the door, the access, the terrifying scope.\r\n[L9] He opened his eyes.\r\n[L10] “Purity,” he said, and the word tasted like both prayer and chain. “If I’m anything, I’m that. Because what I felt wasn’t destruction. It wasn’t shaping. It wasn’t harmony. It was… cleansing force. A current. A pressure. A straightening.”\r\n[L11] Kerrin nodded, slow. “Purity aligns.”\r\n[L12] Mobel’s jaw tightened. “Purity also burns.”\r\n[L13] Kerrin didn’t disagree.\r\n[L14] Mobel’s fingers curled once on his knee, a small involuntary grip like he was holding a handle that wasn’t there.\r\n[L15] He pictured the ocean again, obeying. He pictured taking it and setting it down gentle as cloth. He pictured failing and drowning the city anyway.\r\n[L16] He tasted metallic again, and this time the annoyance rose hot.\r\n[L17] Stupid metal.\r\n[L18] He forced a breath on the slack phase.\r\n[L19] In. Out.\r\n[L20] Then he looked at Kerrin.\r\n[L21] “If it happens again,” Mobel said, “don’t let me get pretty with it.”\r\n[L22] Kerrin’s expression didn’t shift. “Pretty.”\r\n[L23] “Don’t let me make a show,” Mobel said. “Don’t let me forget what I am.”\r\n[L24] Kerrin’s answer was immediate. Familiar. A promise given before, in different forms.\r\n[L25] “I won’t,” he said.\r\n[L26] Outside, the terrace worklines kept moving. Stone passed hand to hand. Names called. Rope tightened. The world held itself together with labor and contradiction.\r\n[L27] Mobel sat at the edge of the room and listened to the doubled hum inside the wall, and tried to decide whether feeling alive was a blessing or just a different kind of sentence.\r\n[L28] Chapter 11 - Tiffani (The Cadence)\r\n[L29] The bells shifted cadence.\r\n[L30] Three short strikes. A pause. One long strike. Two short.\r\n[L31] Stone carried it clean. Pipe ribs carried it wrong, vibrating the pattern into extra notes. The doubled hum laid under the metal like a second hand trying to write over the first.\r\n[L32] People stopped moving. Heads turned toward the nearest station tower.\r\n[L33] Tiffani stopped too. Slate under her arm. Pencil in her fingers. She counted the spacing, not the meaning.\r\n[L34] Three. Pause. Long. Two.\r\n[L35] Station notice.\r\n[L36] Not collapse warning. Not fire. Not brace call.\r\n[L37] The long strike meant: listen.\r\n[L38] A runner climbed the tower steps and pinned a sheet to the board below the bell yoke. Another runner held the corners when the wind pulled.\r\n[L39] Tiffani watched the paper flap, then watched the hands that pinned it. Copper-gold grit lifted along the slate edge and settled again.\r\n[L40] Jhace was two paces to her right. Wedge bar on his shoulder like a yoke. His hands stayed low. Visible.\r\n[L41] Chloen stayed close, small shadow tucked into the gap between their bodies and the crowd.\r\n[L42] The bell tower runner shouted.\r\n[L43] \"By Crown order. Assembly at the Basin Stadium. Live address. Corlexi of Infrastructure.\n# 【6†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file6\nMclick Target: \"22:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Bram didn’t clap, but his shoulders loosened like he’d set a pack down.\r\n[L2] Tiffani finally wrote.\r\n[L3] Just one line.\r\n[L4] Chloen couldn’t see the words, but she saw the graphite move around a darker smear already on the slate, like water avoiding oil.\r\n[L5] Jhace turned away.\r\n[L6] Not far. Just enough.\r\n[L7] Like he was done listening.\r\n[L8] Chloen stared at Eries and tried to figure out what felt wrong.\r\n[L9] It wasn’t the words. The words made sense.\r\n[L10] It was the way they fit too easily. Like a puzzle with pieces shaved down so they’d stop resisting.\r\n[L11] Chloen looked at the emblems stitched into coats and pinned to collars across the crowd. Square. Drop. Loop. Fracture.\r\n[L12] They weren’t where they’d been before.\r\n[L13] She didn’t know how she knew that.\r\n[L14] She just did.\r\n[L15] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\r\n[L16] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\r\n[L17] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\r\n[L18] Tiffani didn’t look down.\r\n[L19] But her pencil stopped.\r\n[L20] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\r\n[L21] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\r\n[L22] Camera (baseline): The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\r\n[L23] Sensory intrusion: The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear. Timing problem. Two beats where there should have been one.\r\n[L24] Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.\r\n[L25] She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.\r\n[L26] Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.\r\n[L27] People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.\r\n[L28] That was new.\r\n[L29] That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.\r\n[L30] Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.\r\n[L31] He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.\r\n[L32] Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.\r\n[L33] Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.\r\n[L34] The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.\r\n[L35] Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day.\n# 【7†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file7\nMclick Target: \"22:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Jhace did not move toward the blood. He watched the brace timber and the shifting stone and the way the stutter had changed the crowd's balance. His hands flexed once, then clenched.\r\n[L2] Chloen stood with her arms tight around herself, eyes on the aisle line. Her mouth worked once as if the metallic taste was too loud.\r\n[L3] Corlexi remained on the platform.\r\n[L4] He did not return to the podium.\r\n[L5] He did not speak.\r\n[L6] His clean coat did not change.\r\n[L7] A registrar leaned in to say something to him. Corlexi nodded once, sharp, and pointed toward the side table.\r\n[L8] The ledger opened.\r\n[L9] Names began to be taken.\r\n[L10] Tiffani closed her slate.\r\n[L11] Not because the blood was not data.\r\n[L12] Because the act of writing had just become part of the event.\r\n[L13] She could feel the graphite under her fingers like a conductor.\r\n[L14] She stood and shifted one step to spare her knee.\r\n[L15] She did not look at Bram.\r\n[L16] She did not look at Jhace.\r\n[L17] She looked at the horn-glass amplifiers and the copper wire bracing and the way the wire shivered on the baseline as if it was listening.\r\n[L18] Then she exhaled on the slack phase and moved toward the aisle line where the blood had been, because measurement required proximity and proximity now carried cost.\r\n[L19] Chapter 15 – Tiffani (The Omission)\r\n[L20] The bells rang the Assembly cadence.\r\n[L21] Not alarm. Not evacuation. Not mourning.\r\n[L22] Instruction.\r\n[L23] Three low. One high. Pause. Then the repeat—slightly off, like everything else now. The harmonic bled into the bronze, stretched the spacing between notes until the rhythm felt learned rather than remembered.\r\n[L24] Tiffani stood where she could see the whole of it without being seen herself.\r\n[L25] The crowd had been arranged for sound, not comfort. That was deliberate. Stone risers angled shallow toward the dais. Rope lines kept bodies from pressing too close to the glasswork that carried the amplification. Brace captains flanked the aisles, hands loose, eyes working.\r\n[L26] People had stopped talking before the bells finished.\r\n[L27] That mattered.\r\n[L28] They were waiting to be told what the Event was.\r\n[L29] Corlexi stood already at the center of the platform when the last echo died. No procession. No flourish. He wore Crown gray—clean lines, no ornament—and the way he held himself said he had practiced stillness until it read as calm instead of fear.\r\n[L30] Tiffani opened her slate.\r\n[L31] Not to write yet. To feel the grit along the edge lift and settle. Baseline check. It answered.\r\n[L32] Corlexi began.\r\n[L33] “Citizens of Saint Merro and the upper terraces,” he said, voice carried clean through the glass horns. “We are convened under conditions without precedent.”\r\n[L34] That was true. Also useless.\r\n[L35] “We have experienced a planetary structural failure event,” he continued. “External in origin. Sudden. Unpreventable.”\r\n[L36] External. Tiffani underlined it in her mind and waited for the evidence that would follow.\r\n[L37] “It has compromised the grid,” Corlexi said. “Altered pressure dynamics. Introduced sustained harmonic instability.”\r\n[L38] He was careful with verbs. Passive constructions. No actor named.\r\n[L39] The crowd stayed quiet. Too quiet. People leaned forward a fraction with each pulse, then settled back on the slack. They had learned the rhythm faster than she’d expected.\r\n[L40] Corlexi spoke of logistics.\r\n[L41] Assembly points. Rationing. Repair priorities. The need for order while assessment continued. He named the glass-houses—plural—and acknowledged the Underface without promising aid he couldn’t deliver.\r\n[L42] All of it was competent.\r\n[L43] All of it was insufficient.\r\n[L44] Tiffani waited for him to say how big.\r\n[L45] She waited for where else.\n# 【8†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file8\nMclick Target: \"22:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Familiar. A promise given before, in different forms.\r\n[L2] “I won’t,” he said.\r\n[L3] Outside, the terrace worklines kept moving. Stone passed hand to hand. Names called. Rope tightened. The world held itself together with labor and contradiction.\r\n[L4] Mobel sat at the edge of the room and listened to the doubled hum inside the wall, and tried to decide whether feeling alive was a blessing or just a different kind of sentence.\r\n[L5] Chapter 11 - Tiffani (The Cadence)\r\n[L6] The bells shifted cadence.\r\n[L7] Three short strikes. A pause. One long strike. Two short.\r\n[L8] Stone carried it clean. Pipe ribs carried it wrong, vibrating the pattern into extra notes. The doubled hum laid under the metal like a second hand trying to write over the first.\r\n[L9] People stopped moving. Heads turned toward the nearest station tower.\r\n[L10] Tiffani stopped too. Slate under her arm. Pencil in her fingers. She counted the spacing, not the meaning.\r\n[L11] Three. Pause. Long. Two.\r\n[L12] Station notice.\r\n[L13] Not collapse warning. Not fire. Not brace call.\r\n[L14] The long strike meant: listen.\r\n[L15] A runner climbed the tower steps and pinned a sheet to the board below the bell yoke. Another runner held the corners when the wind pulled.\r\n[L16] Tiffani watched the paper flap, then watched the hands that pinned it. Copper-gold grit lifted along the slate edge and settled again.\r\n[L17] Jhace was two paces to her right. Wedge bar on his shoulder like a yoke. His hands stayed low. Visible.\r\n[L18] Chloen stayed close, small shadow tucked into the gap between their bodies and the crowd.\r\n[L19] The bell tower runner shouted.\r\n[L20] \"By Crown order. Assembly at the Basin Stadium. Live address. Corlexi of Infrastructure. Two hours from first notice. All able bodies. Bring injured if transportable. Bring names for allocation.\"\r\n[L21] A second runner repeated it down the street, voice already hoarse.\r\n[L22] Tiffani did not write it. She copied the timing in her head.\r\n[L23] Corlexi.\r\n[L24] She had heard the name once in the glass-house, spoken with irritation by an older observer who disliked administrators. She had never seen him.\r\n[L25] The crowd broke into motion in fragments.\r\n[L26] People argued. People complied. People dragged carts. People carried children. Rope lines formed across cracks as if the street itself had become a ledger.\r\n[L27] Tiffani stepped to the slack phase and walked.\r\n[L28] Jhace moved with her without asking.\r\n[L29] Chloen moved because the bodies around her moved.\r\n[L30] At the next station, the same cadence rang. The same sheet hung. The same order.\r\n[L31] The Crown could not speak across the grid anymore, but it could still ring metal.\r\n[L32] Tiffani tracked the failures that made that true.\r\n[L33] Long-range line: dead. The doubled harmonic rode the pipes and ate signal. Brass joints chattered under the pulse. Copper runs sang into noise.\r\n[L34] Local bell: usable. Barely.\r\n[L35] Messenger: human bandwidth.\r\n[L36] She kept those columns in her head the way she kept star positions.\r\n[L37] They reached a wider street where the terraces flattened and the stone was less fractured. A guard post had been set at the corner with a temporary marker: a pole, a hanging lantern, a scrap of white cloth tied in a knot.\r\n[L38] A young guard stood beside it. Crown colors on his sash, but the sash was too new, the stitching too bright. Helmet under one arm. Eyes tired anyway.\r\n[L39] He lifted a hand to stop the flow, then thought better of it and stepped aside to guide instead.\r\n[L40] \"Stadium route,\" he called. \"Keep to the marked flats. Avoid the red chalk.\n# 【9†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn22file9\nMclick Target: \"22:9\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Then, quieter: “Force applied.”\r\n[L2] Jhace swallowed copper. It scratched.\r\n[L3] Chloen leaned forward, gripping the broken parapet stone with both hands like she could keep herself from falling into the sight of it. Her knuckles were white under dust.\r\n[L4] “Did… did the water—” she started.\r\n[L5] “It turned,” Jhace said. Flat. Confirming. No comfort.\r\n[L6] Chloen blinked hard and looked between the redirected basin and the city that had just not died.\r\n[L7] “How?”\r\n[L8] Jhace didn’t answer because he didn’t have one that wasn’t a lie.\r\n[L9] Tiffani did not look up from her slate. “Not wind,” she said, as if correcting herself before anyone else could. “Not terrain. Too late. Too clean.”\r\n[L10] Jhace’s jaw worked. He tasted metal again.\r\n[L11] He imagined his own hands doing it.\r\n[L12] His sternum tightened like the thought alone tried to pull the line into existence. Heat climbed under his ribs.\r\n[L13] He forced his breath into a measured pull on the slack phase. In. Out.\r\n[L14] He did not move his hands.\r\n[L15] Down in the basin, the diverted wave slammed into rock and scrub and the underside of lower terraces. The impact threw up a fan of mist that glittered with copper-gold flecks even at this distance, catching the kindling-light in flashes that made the air look full of filings.\r\n[L16] Jhace watched the mist drift and settle and watched, for one heartbeat, a section of spray hang wrong—suspended, slow—before gravity reclaimed it.\r\n[L17] Field residue.\r\n[L18] A use.\r\n[L19] A cost.\r\n[L20] Somewhere down there, someone was paying.\r\n[L21] Jhace turned his head enough to look at Tiffani’s slate at last—not to read it, but to see where she was looking.\r\n[L22] Her pencil stopped for a single beat.\r\n[L23] She wrote one new line, crisp as a stamped mark:\r\n[L24] M3: Mass-water vector deviation. Subject unknown.\r\n[L25] Then she added, smaller:\r\n[L26] Applied at last-second proximity.\r\n[L27] Jhace stared at the words and felt his hunger sharpen into direction.\r\n[L28] Somebody near the waterline. Somebody close enough to get drowned if they missed. Somebody who had touched the ocean and lived.\r\n[L29] He looked back down the face, scanning for a human scale point in the chaos. Too far. Too much spray. Too many moving pieces.\r\n[L30] Chloen’s voice was small. “Is it… like what you did?”\r\n[L31] Jhace’s hands flexed once, involuntary. The tremor tried to rise.\r\n[L32] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\r\n[L33] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\r\n[L34] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\r\n[L35] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\r\n[L36] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\r\n[L37] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\r\n[L38] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\r\n[L39] His sternum was already too awake.\r\n[L40] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\r\n[L41] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\r\n[L42] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\r\n[L43] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\r\n[L44] And now he knew it could be done.\r\n[L45] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\r\n[L46] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\r\n[L47] Not because there was nothing to write.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",

          "result": "# 【0†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file0\nMclick Target: \"10:0\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Just enough that Chloen saw it.\r\n[L2] Tiffani did not nod.\r\n[L3] But she didn’t lower the slate either.\r\n[L4] Jhace’s mouth tightened. He shook his head once, sharp, like knocking something loose.\r\n[L5] Eries saw him. Chloen was sure of it. But she didn’t look at him. She looked at the crowd instead.\r\n[L6] “There are those among you,” Eries said, “who have already proven capable. Not through training. Not through doctrine. Through instinct.”\r\n[L7] Chloen’s stomach dipped.\r\n[L8] “You don’t need registries,” Eries went on smoothly. “You don’t need lists. You don’t need to be afraid of being counted.”\r\n[L9] Chloen thought of the woman with the chalk. The counting. The eyes.\r\n[L10] Eries’ voice softened.\r\n[L11] “You need support. Structure. Permission.”\r\n[L12] That word slid into Chloen like a splinter.\r\n[L13] Permission was something adults gave you so they didn’t have to watch.\r\n[L14] Bram shifted his spear to his other hand. He looked at Tiffani, quick, like he wanted to check something.\r\n[L15] “This isn’t about power,” Eries said. “It’s about responsibility.”\r\n[L16] Chloen snorted before she could stop herself. It came out small, like a breath that tripped.\r\n[L17] The woman next to her glanced down. Chloen stared at the stone until the woman looked away.\r\n[L18] Tiffani’s pencil touched the slate.\r\n[L19] Just touched.\r\n[L20] Didn’t move.\r\n[L21] Chloen saw the moment stretch. Like the space between one count and the next when you didn’t know if the rhythm would come back.\r\n[L22] Eries finished gently.\r\n[L23] “We can build something that holds,” she said. “Together. Or we can pretend the ground isn’t still moving.”\r\n[L24] Silence sat on the crowd.\r\n[L25] Then someone clapped.\r\n[L26] Then someone else.\r\n[L27] Bram didn’t clap, but his shoulders loosened like he’d set a pack down.\r\n[L28] Tiffani finally wrote.\r\n[L29] Just one line.\r\n[L30] Chloen couldn’t see the words, but she saw the graphite move around a darker smear already on the slate, like water avoiding oil.\r\n[L31] Jhace turned away.\r\n[L32] Not far. Just enough.\r\n[L33] Like he was done listening.\r\n[L34] Chloen stared at Eries and tried to figure out what felt wrong.\r\n[L35] It wasn’t the words. The words made sense.\r\n[L36] It was the way they fit too easily. Like a puzzle with pieces shaved down so they’d stop resisting.\r\n[L37] Chloen looked at the emblems stitched into coats and pinned to collars across the crowd. Square. Drop. Loop. Fracture.\r\n[L38] They weren’t where they’d been before.\r\n[L39] She didn’t know how she knew that.\r\n[L40] She just did.\r\n[L41] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\r\n[L42] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\r\n[L43] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\r\n[L44] Tiffani didn’t look down.\r\n[L45] But her pencil stopped.\r\n[L46] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\r\n[L47] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\r\n[L48] Camera (baseline): The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\r\n[L49] Sensory intrusion: The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear. Timing problem.\n# 【1†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file1\nMclick Target: \"10:1\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The tremor tried to rise.\n[L2] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\n[L3] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\n[L4] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\n[L5] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\n[L6] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\n[L7] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\n[L8] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\n[L9] His sternum was already too awake.\n[L10] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\n[L11] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\n[L12] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\n[L13] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\n[L14] And now he knew it could be done.\n[L15] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\n[L16] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\n[L17] Not because there was nothing to write. Because there was too much, and the act of recording would change what people thought they were doing. If she raised graphite now, they’d start performing their panic for the page. They’d turn grief into testimony.\n[L18] She wasn’t ready to be that kind of authority.\n[L19] The upper terrace had become a sorting place.\n[L20] Not formally or by decree—by physics.\n[L21] Rubble moved downhill when it could. People moved uphill when they could breathe. Anyone who could still walk drifted toward the wider stone flats where the terraces broadened and the parapets were intact enough to lean on. The broken streets below funneled survivors toward this ledge the way water found low points.\n[L22] Volunteers—if you could call them that—had formed lines. Hands passed stone from hand to hand. Smaller pieces went into baskets. Larger slabs were propped with scavenged timber and wedge bars. Someone had tied a length of rope across a crack in the paving to keep the crowd from stepping onto a section that shifted on the doubled pulse.\n[L23] The work sounded ordinary.\n[L24] Stone scraping. Breath. A cough. A child’s thin crying. A man’s voice calling names into the air until his throat went raw.\n[L25] Ordinary work inside an impossible day.\n[L26] Tiffani moved through it without stepping into anyone’s line. Her eyes tracked loads and fractures the way her hands tracked stars. She counted by seeing.\n[L27] One: a retaining wall seam opened a hair each pulse.\n[L28] Two: a lintel with a new shear line, chalked in red by someone who knew what “don’t stand here” meant.\n[L29] Three: a brace post that had set crooked after liquefaction—compensating now, but only by being loud about it.\n[L30] She was not the only one paying attention. That was the most surprising thing.\n[L31] A woman with a carpenter’s hammer had marked a series of doorframes with wax. A boy with a long pole tapped stones and listened for the hollow note of delamination. Someone—older, with a scarf over their mouth—had turned a smashed cart into a supply table and was rationing water in cups like it mattered, because it did.\n[L32] Tiffani felt the doubled hum in her knee with every step. It registered as a timing problem: the joint wanted one rhythm and the ground insisted on two.\n[L33] She adjusted her stride to the slack phase. It made walking possible.\n[L34] She did not allow herself to enjoy that small mastery.\n# 【2†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file2\nMclick Target: \"10:2\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Two. A cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.\r\n[L2] Three. Rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.\r\n[L3] Four. A pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.\r\n[L4] The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.\r\n[L5] When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she’d been taught and hated that she’d been taught anything today.\r\n[L6] She heard Tiffani’s voice before she saw her.\r\n[L7] Not loud. Not comforting. Just clear.\r\n[L8] “Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped.”\r\n[L9] Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.\r\n[L10] Tiffani was a few paces away in the semicircle that had broken into work groups. Slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn’t trust it.\r\n[L11] Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone.\r\n[L12] “Someone moved the ocean,” Jhace said, and even though Chloen had already watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.\r\n[L13] Someone else answered—one of the important men with rings, maybe, or one of the brace captains. Chloen couldn’t see who. She could hear the shape of the words.\r\n[L14] “Not possible.”\r\n[L15] “It happened,” Jhace said.\r\n[L16] Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.\r\n[L17] Tiffani said, “We measure,” and Chloen didn’t know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn’t fix.\r\n[L18] Jhace’s voice lowered. “And if the slate woman shows up again—”\r\n[L19] Slate woman.\r\n[L20] Chloen’s skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace’s hands.\r\n[L21] Chloen didn’t like her. She didn’t know her. She didn’t like the way the woman’s attention had felt like fingers.\r\n[L22] Tiffani’s pencil tapped once. A small sound. A decision sound.\r\n[L23] Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.\r\n[L24] She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.\r\n[L25] The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.\r\n[L26] Her throat tightened.\r\n[L27] She kept it to herself.\r\n[L28] Aunt Lessa most likely dead.\r\n[L29] No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn’t bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.\r\n[L30] Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else.\r\n[L31] “Okay,” she told herself again, quieter.\r\n[L32] Then, because she didn’t know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn’t believe, but needed anyway.\r\n[L33] “Still here.”\r\n[L34] And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.\r\n[L35] Not crying.\r\n[L36] Not speaking.\r\n[L37] Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.\r\n[L38] Chapter 10 — Mobel (The Shard)\r\n[L39] Mobel didn’t sit in the center of the room.\r\n[L40] Old habit. Old math.\r\n[L41] Center meant eyes. Eyes meant judgment. Judgment meant cages.\n# 【3†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file3\nMclick Target: \"10:3\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Then, quieter: “Force applied.”\r\n[L2] Jhace swallowed copper. It scratched.\r\n[L3] Chloen leaned forward, gripping the broken parapet stone with both hands like she could keep herself from falling into the sight of it. Her knuckles were white under dust.\r\n[L4] “Did… did the water—” she started.\r\n[L5] “It turned,” Jhace said. Flat. Confirming. No comfort.\r\n[L6] Chloen blinked hard and looked between the redirected basin and the city that had just not died.\r\n[L7] “How?”\r\n[L8] Jhace didn’t answer because he didn’t have one that wasn’t a lie.\r\n[L9] Tiffani did not look up from her slate. “Not wind,” she said, as if correcting herself before anyone else could. “Not terrain. Too late. Too clean.”\r\n[L10] Jhace’s jaw worked. He tasted metal again.\r\n[L11] He imagined his own hands doing it.\r\n[L12] His sternum tightened like the thought alone tried to pull the line into existence. Heat climbed under his ribs.\r\n[L13] He forced his breath into a measured pull on the slack phase. In. Out.\r\n[L14] He did not move his hands.\r\n[L15] Down in the basin, the diverted wave slammed into rock and scrub and the underside of lower terraces. The impact threw up a fan of mist that glittered with copper-gold flecks even at this distance, catching the kindling-light in flashes that made the air look full of filings.\r\n[L16] Jhace watched the mist drift and settle and watched, for one heartbeat, a section of spray hang wrong—suspended, slow—before gravity reclaimed it.\r\n[L17] Field residue.\r\n[L18] A use.\r\n[L19] A cost.\r\n[L20] Somewhere down there, someone was paying.\r\n[L21] Jhace turned his head enough to look at Tiffani’s slate at last—not to read it, but to see where she was looking.\r\n[L22] Her pencil stopped for a single beat.\r\n[L23] She wrote one new line, crisp as a stamped mark:\r\n[L24] M3: Mass-water vector deviation. Subject unknown.\r\n[L25] Then she added, smaller:\r\n[L26] Applied at last-second proximity.\r\n[L27] Jhace stared at the words and felt his hunger sharpen into direction.\r\n[L28] Somebody near the waterline. Somebody close enough to get drowned if they missed. Somebody who had touched the ocean and lived.\r\n[L29] He looked back down the face, scanning for a human scale point in the chaos. Too far. Too much spray. Too many moving pieces.\r\n[L30] Chloen’s voice was small. “Is it… like what you did?”\r\n[L31] Jhace’s hands flexed once, involuntary. The tremor tried to rise.\r\n[L32] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\r\n[L33] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\r\n[L34] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\r\n[L35] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\r\n[L36] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\r\n[L37] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\r\n[L38] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\r\n[L39] His sternum was already too awake.\r\n[L40] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\r\n[L41] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\r\n[L42] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\r\n[L43] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\r\n[L44] And now he knew it could be done.\r\n[L45] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\r\n[L46] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\r\n[L47] Not because there was nothing to write.\n# 【4†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file4\nMclick Target: \"10:4\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] As he did, he glanced—not toward the crowd, not toward the glass-houses—but toward the far edge of the dais.\n[L2] Toward a woman already standing.\n[L3] Eries did not wait for permission.\n[L4] She did not raise her voice.\n[L5] She did not step into the amplification.\n[L6] She simply spoke, and the room leaned toward her as if sound had weight.\n[L7] “Administrator Corlexi,” she said, clear and calm, “has thanked you for your trust.”\n[L8] Corlexi continued offstage.\n[L9] Eries faced the crowd.\n[L10] “And I would like to thank you,” she went on, “for your silence.”\n[L11] Tiffani felt the room change shape.\n[L12] Not louder. Not quieter.\n[L13] Aligned.\n[L14] Eries smiled—not kindly, not cruelly. Precisely. Like someone who knew the cost of the truth and intended to spend it anyway.\n[L15] “And now,” Eries said, “I would like to speak about what we have not yet named.”\n[L16] Tiffani’s pencil touched the slate.\n[L17] And for the first time since the bells rang, she did not know whether she was about to record data—\n[L18] —or watch it be taken.\n[L19] Chapter 16 – Chloen (The Easy Answer)\n[L20] Chloen saw it before the words changed.\n[L21] Not the woman. Not the sound.\n[L22] The slate.\n[L23] Tiffani’s hand moved the way it always did right before something mattered. Not fast. Not careful. Just… ready. Like the slate had weight and she was deciding whether to lift it or let it stay where it was.\n[L24] Chloen watched the edge of it. The grit there lifted a little, then settled. Lifted again.\n[L25] She felt it in her teeth.\n[L26] The lady on the platform hadn’t stepped into the loud-glass, but her voice still got everywhere. It slid into the space between people and sat there like it belonged.\n[L27] “And now,” the lady said, “I would like to speak about what we have not yet named.”\n[L28] People leaned without noticing.\n[L29] Chloen leaned too, then stopped when she realized she was doing it and hugged her elbows instead. She didn’t want to move unless she meant it.\n[L30] The lady—Eries, someone whispered nearby, like names were currency now—looked like she’d always been standing there. Dark dress. Bright pin at the collar, shaped like a broken loop. She smiled like she was sorry for everyone, but not enough to stop.\n[L31] “You’ve been told this was external,” Eries said. “Unpreventable. Finished.”\n[L32] Her eyes moved across the crowd, slow and even, like she was counting heads without numbers.\n[L33] “I am here to tell you,” she continued, “that this is not over—and pretending otherwise is not stability. It’s delay.”\n[L34] Chloen felt a small, sharp click inside her chest.\n[L35] Delay was a word you used when something bad was coming back.\n[L36] Around her, grown-ups shifted. Some nodded. Some crossed their arms like that might hold something in.\n[L37] Eries didn’t raise her voice.\n[L38] “You have felt the cost,” she said. “In your mouths. In your joints. In your breath. You have seen stone obey hands. You have seen water turn.”\n[L39] Chloen thought of the wave. The way it bent like it had listened.\n[L40] Jhace didn’t move. He looked like a wall someone had leaned on too long.\n[L41] Bram did move. Just a little. Like he’d been holding something in and finally found a place to put it.\n[L42] “That frightens you,” Eries said. “It should.”\n[L43] Chloen frowned.\n[L44] That wasn’t how you talked if you were trying to help.\n[L45] “But fear,” Eries went on, “is not the enemy. Confusion is.”\n[L46] Tiffani’s pencil hovered. Not writing. Waiting.\n# 【5†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file5\nMclick Target: \"10:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The person collapsed.\n[L2] The lintel fell harmlessly because the people under it had cleared in that half-breath.\n[L3] Tiffani didn’t let it become myth. She let it become a list.\n[L4] M1: Jhace — slab suspension — tremor + copper surge + sternum lock.\n[L5] M2: unknown — lintel arrest — collapse.\n[L6] Space left for more.\n[L7] Across the sky, the orbital band brightened as the sun angle shifted, making the debris flash like a scar being shown off.\n[L8] Tiffani stopped long enough to look at the Rift again: black in blue, unchanged.\n[L9] She hummed once, steady, and wrote the last line on that slate page:\n[L10] New baseline established.\n[L11] Then she turned away from the horizon and back toward the city’s breaking points, because whatever the sky had become, people were still made of stone and water and breath—and those were failing in ways she could, at least sometimes, predict.\n[L12] Chapter 4 — Jhace (Firebreak)\n[L13] Jhace kept moving because stopping made the shaking worse.\n[L14] Not always or forever—just right now.\n[L15] The bell cadence pulled him through the quarter like a hook in the ribs—fast peals from Brace Street, then the staggered answering ring from a lane he knew by smell more than signage. The kit strap sawed at his shoulder. His hands wouldn’t hold still on the wedge bar, so he let them hang and used his legs.\n[L16] Copper still sat on his tongue. Every swallow scraped.\n[L17] The doubled hum underfoot stayed. It wasn’t an aftershock. It was a new layer to the world, pressed through stone, through pipe, through bone. The city’s old pipe-thrum was still there, but it had company now, and the company didn’t know how to behave.\n[L18] People ran in bursts—start, freeze, start again—like the ground kept asking their bodies a question they couldn’t answer. A man with a basket slammed into Jhace’s shoulder and spun away without apologizing. A woman hauled a child by the sleeve, the kid’s feet skidding, eyes wide.\n[L19] Jhace pointed once, short and sharp. “Open ground. Away from façades.”\n[L20] Some listened. Most didn’t. Bells made people think there was one safe direction. Bells were a lie on a day like this.\n[L21] He cut left at the corner by the tannery vents—hot, sour air—and almost ran into a knot of bodies jammed in the street. Not staring at the horizon now. Staring at something immediate.\n[L22] Fire.\n[L23] A building at the far end of the lane had lit up like a lantern. Two windows vomiting orange, smoke rolling out under the eaves in thick, oily layers. The flames weren’t tall; they were hungry and spreading sideways along old wood and pitch. Heat shimmered above the door like glass trying to become water.\n[L24] The lane was a choke point: stone walls tight, terraces stacked, one way out blocked by that burning front.\n[L25] People were pressed against each other, coughing, trying to turn around in a space that didn’t have room. Some were trying to push through anyway, as if speed could argue with flame.\n[L26] Jhace’s brain stopped seeing “fire” and started seeing constraints.\n[L27] Narrow lane. Wind pulling toward the Exit Face in pulses. Smoke already leaning. Limited exits. Water sources? Pipe mains? Steam vents?\n[L28] He ran.\n[L29] Not into the crowd—into the edges, where he could move. He shoved through a gap, shoulder checking a man who was frozen mid-step. “Back,” he snapped. “Back from the door. You’ll feed it.”\n[L30] A woman screamed at him, words useless. He didn’t answer.\n# 【6†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file6\nMclick Target: \"10:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Familiar. A promise given before, in different forms.\r\n[L2] “I won’t,” he said.\r\n[L3] Outside, the terrace worklines kept moving. Stone passed hand to hand. Names called. Rope tightened. The world held itself together with labor and contradiction.\r\n[L4] Mobel sat at the edge of the room and listened to the doubled hum inside the wall, and tried to decide whether feeling alive was a blessing or just a different kind of sentence.\r\n[L5] Chapter 11 - Tiffani (The Cadence)\r\n[L6] The bells shifted cadence.\r\n[L7] Three short strikes. A pause. One long strike. Two short.\r\n[L8] Stone carried it clean. Pipe ribs carried it wrong, vibrating the pattern into extra notes. The doubled hum laid under the metal like a second hand trying to write over the first.\r\n[L9] People stopped moving. Heads turned toward the nearest station tower.\r\n[L10] Tiffani stopped too. Slate under her arm. Pencil in her fingers. She counted the spacing, not the meaning.\r\n[L11] Three. Pause. Long. Two.\r\n[L12] Station notice.\r\n[L13] Not collapse warning. Not fire. Not brace call.\r\n[L14] The long strike meant: listen.\r\n[L15] A runner climbed the tower steps and pinned a sheet to the board below the bell yoke. Another runner held the corners when the wind pulled.\r\n[L16] Tiffani watched the paper flap, then watched the hands that pinned it. Copper-gold grit lifted along the slate edge and settled again.\r\n[L17] Jhace was two paces to her right. Wedge bar on his shoulder like a yoke. His hands stayed low. Visible.\r\n[L18] Chloen stayed close, small shadow tucked into the gap between their bodies and the crowd.\r\n[L19] The bell tower runner shouted.\r\n[L20] \"By Crown order. Assembly at the Basin Stadium. Live address. Corlexi of Infrastructure. Two hours from first notice. All able bodies. Bring injured if transportable. Bring names for allocation.\"\r\n[L21] A second runner repeated it down the street, voice already hoarse.\r\n[L22] Tiffani did not write it. She copied the timing in her head.\r\n[L23] Corlexi.\r\n[L24] She had heard the name once in the glass-house, spoken with irritation by an older observer who disliked administrators. She had never seen him.\r\n[L25] The crowd broke into motion in fragments.\r\n[L26] People argued. People complied. People dragged carts. People carried children. Rope lines formed across cracks as if the street itself had become a ledger.\r\n[L27] Tiffani stepped to the slack phase and walked.\r\n[L28] Jhace moved with her without asking.\r\n[L29] Chloen moved because the bodies around her moved.\r\n[L30] At the next station, the same cadence rang. The same sheet hung. The same order.\r\n[L31] The Crown could not speak across the grid anymore, but it could still ring metal.\r\n[L32] Tiffani tracked the failures that made that true.\r\n[L33] Long-range line: dead. The doubled harmonic rode the pipes and ate signal. Brass joints chattered under the pulse. Copper runs sang into noise.\r\n[L34] Local bell: usable. Barely.\r\n[L35] Messenger: human bandwidth.\r\n[L36] She kept those columns in her head the way she kept star positions.\r\n[L37] They reached a wider street where the terraces flattened and the stone was less fractured. A guard post had been set at the corner with a temporary marker: a pole, a hanging lantern, a scrap of white cloth tied in a knot.\r\n[L38] A young guard stood beside it. Crown colors on his sash, but the sash was too new, the stitching too bright. Helmet under one arm. Eyes tired anyway.\r\n[L39] He lifted a hand to stop the flow, then thought better of it and stepped aside to guide instead.\r\n[L40] \"Stadium route,\" he called. \"Keep to the marked flats. Avoid the red chalk.\n# 【7†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file7\nMclick Target: \"10:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] Chloen swallowed and tasted metal again—duller now, like it had been there a while.\n[L2] She leaned toward Tiffani’s side, not touching her, just close enough that if Tiffani moved, she’d feel it.\n[L3] “Don’t,” Chloen whispered. Not loud enough for the slate. Just for the space between them.\n[L4] Tiffani didn’t look down.\n[L5] But her pencil stopped.\n[L6] And Chloen watched Eries smile wider, like she’d felt the resistance and counted it anyway.\n[L7] Chapter 17 — Tiffani (Deployment)\n[L8] The stadium held a bowl of bodies. Rope lanes cut the stone flats into corridors. Bell towers at three stations rang cadence through metal throats. Registry tables formed a spine under awnings. Water barrels sat in rows. A thin ash haze drifted and then reversed, as the air pulled and slacked.\n[L9] The doubled hum sat in the knee joint like an offset gear—a timing problem: two beats where there should have been one.\n[L10] Tiffani stood where she could see the lanes and the stage without being part of either.\n[L11] She kept her slate tucked, not open. Open invited requests. Requests turned observation into labor. Labor made her miss the drift.\n[L12] Bells rang again—short, short, long—then a pause, then the same sequence repeated. Not panic cadence. Notice cadence. Central-station call.\n[L13] People reacted like trained metal. Heads lifted. Feet adjusted. Bodies shifted toward the lanes without anyone shouting at them.\n[L14] That was new.\n[L15] That was the first clean thing she’d seen since the sky tore.\n[L16] Bram moved along the nearest rope line with three other crown interns—too young for the way they were trying to carry authority, too stubborn to drop it. He had a sash pinned wrong and kept re-seating it as if fabric could be corrected into competence.\n[L17] He leaned down to a woman with a wrapped arm and pointed toward the nearest water barrel. His mouth moved. The woman nodded, then laughed once—short, unwilling. Bram’s shoulders loosened like he’d removed a pressure pin.\n[L18] Jhace stood off the line, wedge bar still in hand. Not working now. Watching. Refusing to be slotted into a lane.\n[L19] Chloen remained close enough to Tiffani that the crowd didn’t swallow her. Close enough that Tiffani could track her without turning it into a promise. The girl’s eyes kept jumping—rope, table, stage, back to rope—like she was mapping exits.\n[L20] The stage had been a platform for games. It was now a point of control.\n[L21] Corlexi had stepped down and taken a side position near the rear struts, half-shadowed by hanging banners. Form emblem at his collar. Clean coat. Too clean for the day. His posture read administrative fatigue as if it were a uniform requirement.\n[L22] Eries stood in the center.\n[L23] High class by cut and fit alone—no dust-stained hem, no patched seams, no improvised wrap. She wore a mantle that could have been ceremonial if it hadn’t been practical wool. That was the trick. She looked like she belonged and like she’d come to help.\n[L24] She didn’t raise her hands for silence. She waited until the bell cadence finished and let the last long note die.\n[L25] The stadium quieted on its own.\n[L26] Tiffani catalogued the quiet like a structural event.\n[L27] Eries spoke without amplification and still carried.\n[L28] “Your air is changing,” she said, and didn’t dress it in myth. “It will keep changing. The pull and slack will continue until the planet settles into a new baseline.”\n[L29] Baseline. Useful word. Correct enough to calm.\n# 【8†The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt†file_0000000023fc7230b413bd6905a776b9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: The Confluence Chronicles (Chapters 1-18 & Epilogue).txt\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file8\nMclick Target: \"10:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] You are a person. But it helps me remember.\"\r\n[L2] Tiffani tracked the accuracy: coarse, but usable.\r\n[L3] Jhace watched the street edges. Load paths. Crack marks. Rope lines. He moved like he was always half a step away from catching something.\r\n[L4] Chloen watched Bram instead. Not the words. The hands. He kept them open. Visible.\r\n[L5] They reached the last ramp down to the stadium bowl. The crowd density increased. Rope lines became lanes. Guard posts multiplied. Someone had hung glass horns on a scaffold to carry a speaker's voice.\r\n[L6] Bram slowed and pointed with two fingers.\r\n[L7] \"Stay together,\" he said. Then, as if he couldn't stop himself, \"Unless you want to try the thrilling sport of getting lost in a disaster crowd. It's like hide-and-seek except the seeker is bureaucracy.\"\r\n[L8] Jhace's eyes cut toward Chloen.\r\n[L9] \"Where'd you come from anyway?\" Bram asked, still in that half-joking tone. \"Where's your mum? Your guardian?\"\r\n[L10] Chloen went still.\r\n[L11] Tiffani felt the change before she saw it. The air around the girl tightened. The grit on her slate edge lifted and did not settle on the first pulse.\r\n[L12] Bram's grin faltered as the question landed and failed to be a joke.\r\n[L13] Chapter 12 - Chloen / Bram (The Question)\r\n[L14] Chloen could still feel the word guardian like a thumb pressed into a bruise.\r\n[L15] It was not the first time someone had asked.\r\n[L16] Usually it was a neighbor. A shopkeeper. A teacher who wanted a signature.\r\n[L17] Today it was a guard with a joke face.\r\n[L18] She didn't look at Bram. Looking at people made them real, and real people asked follow-ups.\r\n[L19] She looked at his boots instead. Leather cracked at the toe. Ash in the seams. A smear of blue paint on the left heel.\r\n[L20] She tried to answer in the smallest way.\r\n[L21] \"Lost,\" she said.\r\n[L22] The word came out flat. Not crying. Not lying. Not telling.\r\n[L23] Bram blinked. The grin fell off him like it had been hooked on wrong.\r\n[L24] \"Ah,\" he said, and the joke engine in him revved and stalled. \"Right. Sorry. That was... I stepped in it. Like, literally. Like a horse in a market. Except there are no horses. You get it.\" He stopped. \"You don't have to get it.\"\r\n[L25] Jhace made a sound through his nose that might have been a laugh if it had been any other day.\r\n[L26] \"Finish it,\" Jhace said.\r\n[L27] Bram stared at him. \"What?\"\r\n[L28] \"The horse thing,\" Jhace said. \"If you start a stupid metaphor you don't get to leave it bleeding in the street.\"\r\n[L29] Tiffani's jaw tightened. She looked past all of them toward the rope lanes and the scaffold horns.\r\n[L30] Bram opened his mouth, realized the rescue attempt would only make it worse, and tried anyway.\r\n[L31] \"Fine,\" he said. \"I stepped in it like a horse in a market, except the market is a disaster and the horse is me and the...\" He waved a hand. \"The point is: I'm sorry.\"\r\n[L32] Chloen's mouth twitched. She hated that it did. She kept her face still. But a small breath escaped her nose.\r\n[L33] Bram saw it and looked relieved like he'd managed to not make things worse by accident. He had. Barely.\r\n[L34] \"Lost,\" Jhace repeated, softer than his voice had been in the forge. Not gentle. Just acknowledging.\r\n[L35] Chloen nodded once. She did not add aunt.\n# 【9†confluence_novella_revised.md†file_00000000a47071fdaf1ac40e7e480e92】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: confluence_novella_revised.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn10file9\nMclick Target: \"10:9\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] The tremor tried to rise.\n[L2] He kept his eyes on the basin. “Bigger,” he said.\n[L3] Tiffani’s pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.\n[L4] “The same,” she corrected, still not looking at him. “Only more.”\n[L5] Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.\n[L6] Below, the city’s bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new cadence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro’s closer peals.\n[L7] Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.\n[L8] Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.\n[L9] His sternum was already too awake.\n[L10] He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.\n[L11] He was glad Tiffani couldn’t write what he was thinking.\n[L12] He wasn’t glad he was thinking it.\n[L13] Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.\n[L14] And now he knew it could be done.\n[L15] Chapter 7 — Tiffani (Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting)\n[L16] Tiffani kept her slate closed.\n[L17] Not because there was nothing to write. Because there was too much, and the act of recording would change what people thought they were doing. If she raised graphite now, they’d start performing their panic for the page. They’d turn grief into testimony.\n[L18] She wasn’t ready to be that kind of authority.\n[L19] The upper terrace had become a sorting place.\n[L20] Not formally or by decree—by physics.\n[L21] Rubble moved downhill when it could. People moved uphill when they could breathe. Anyone who could still walk drifted toward the wider stone flats where the terraces broadened and the parapets were intact enough to lean on. The broken streets below funneled survivors toward this ledge the way water found low points.\n[L22] Volunteers—if you could call them that—had formed lines. Hands passed stone from hand to hand. Smaller pieces went into baskets. Larger slabs were propped with scavenged timber and wedge bars. Someone had tied a length of rope across a crack in the paving to keep the crowd from stepping onto a section that shifted on the doubled pulse.\n[L23] The work sounded ordinary.\n[L24] Stone scraping. Breath. A cough. A child’s thin crying. A man’s voice calling names into the air until his throat went raw.\n[L25] Ordinary work inside an impossible day.\n[L26] Tiffani moved through it without stepping into anyone’s line. Her eyes tracked loads and fractures the way her hands tracked stars. She counted by seeing.\n[L27] One: a retaining wall seam opened a hair each pulse.\n[L28] Two: a lintel with a new shear line, chalked in red by someone who knew what “don’t stand here” meant.\n[L29] Three: a brace post that had set crooked after liquefaction—compensating now, but only by being loud about it.\n[L30] She was not the only one paying attention. That was the most surprising thing.\n[L31] A woman with a carpenter’s hammer had marked a series of doorframes with wax. A boy with a long pole tapped stones and listened for the hollow note of delamination. Someone—older, with a scarf over their mouth—had turned a smashed cart into a supply table and was rationing water in cups like it mattered, because it did.\n[L32] Tiffani felt the doubled hum in her knee with every step. It registered as a timing problem: the joint wanted one rhythm and the ground insisted on two.\n[L33] She adjusted her stride to the slack phase. It made walking possible.\n[L34] She did not allow herself to enjoy that small mastery.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",
          "summary": null,
          "tether_id": null
        },
